segments.s649-g417: This clause has the verb گشادند (an older/poetic past plural of گشودن ‘to open’ or ‘to burst forth’) placed before its noun phrase, a common poetic word order. هر جای رودی ز آب is the post-verbal noun phrase: هر جای = 'everywhere / at every place', رودی is the (indefinite) subject 'a stream / stream(s)' following the verb, and ز آب ('from the water') is a prepositional complement indicating source; together the clause means 'streams opened/burst forth everywhere from the water.'
segments.s649-g418: زمین شد is literal 'the earth became' (شد = become, past tense). پر از is the idiom 'full of' taking an object introduced by از, so پر از جای آرام و خواب = 'full of places of calm and sleep.' آرام and خواب jointly describe the نوعِ جای (the kinds of places); the copular/transformational structure (شد + predicate) is used instead of a present copula.
segments.s649-g313: بیامد is a simple past verb (he came); خردمند is a noun/adjective 'the wise man' following the verb (typical classical word order can place the verb early); نزد + name (قباد) means 'to/into the presence of' (نزد here = 'before, in the presence of'). The whole chunk states who came and to whom.
segments.s649-g314: چنین گفت is a set phrase 'said thus/so' (چنین = 'thus'); گفت is the past of گفتن 'said' (the provided senses list did not give the verbal sense, so that entry is marked as unmatched). کاین is an archaic/poetic contraction of 'که این' meaning 'that this' or 'may this'; ماه here is a poetic epithet for a beautiful person ('moon' = beautiful one); جفت تو باد uses باد as an optative particle 'may (it be)' (باد = optative), so the clause is a wish: 'May this moon be your pair/mate.'
segments.s649-g315: پسندیدی is a 2nd-person past tense 'you liked/approved'. The conjunction و simply links clauses 'and'. ناگهان is an adverb 'suddenly'. دیدیش is the past verb دیدن with the object enclitic -ش ('you saw her') — classical Persian commonly attaches the object as a clitic to the verb.
segments.s649-g316: بدان سان is a fixed phrase 'in that manner / thus' (ب- here is the prepositional/prefix element forming بدان); که introduces a subordinate clause 'that/which'; دیدی is again 2nd-person past 'you saw'; پسندیدیش repeats the verb 'liked/approved' with a final object clitic -ش 'you liked her' (the provided senses list does not show the matching verbal sense, so that token is marked as unmatched). The whole clause reiterates that the seeing and the liking occurred in the same way.
segments.s649-g317: قباد is the subject (proper name). آن پری‌روی را uses آن 'that' plus پری روی (compound meaning 'fair-faced woman'); را is the definite/object marker marking her as the direct object. پیش خواند is a compound verb 'called forward / summoned (to his presence)' — پیش here indicates 'to the presence of' and خواند is the past of خواندن used as 'to call/summon' in this context.
segments.s649-g318: به زانوی indicates location 'to/onto the knee (lap)'; برنشاند is the past transitive verb 'he seated/placed (her) upon' (برنشاند = caused to sit on). کنداورش appears in the manuscript here but the provided senses ('investigator/excavator') do not match the context; functionally the phrase means he set her on his knee (the unknown token likely forms a possessive or descriptive element and is therefore marked as unmatched).
segments.s649-g319: ابا او is here to be read functionally as 'با او' ('with her'); however the token as given ('ابا') does not match the provided senses and is treated as unmatched. یک انگشتری بود و بس: یک انگشتری بود is 'there was one ring' (بود = past of بودن 'was'); و بس 'and that was all' or 'and merely that' — بس conveys limitation ('only/merely' or 'enough' depending on context), here 'only/just'.
segments.s649-g320: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause 'so that / such that'. ارزش means 'value/worth'. به گیتی 'in the world' (به = preposition, گیتی = world). ندانست is past 'knew not' (negative past); the order here places the verb before the indefinite subject — classical style — and کس in this negative clause functions as 'no one' (sense 1), so the clause means 'that no one in the world knew its worth.'
segments.s649-g257: پرستش (a noun) + همی‌کرد (an archaic habitual/perfective auxiliary) forms a habitual past: 'he used to worship' or 'he would worship.' پیش introduces the locus/person before whom the action happens ('in the presence of'), so پیش قباد = 'before Qobad.' Word order is typical poetic Persian: verb phrase before the locative phrase.
segments.s649-g258: وز here functions as the fused poetic 'و زِ' meaning 'and from' (conjoining + 'from'); آن = 'that' refers back to the foregoing; بد نکرد ایچ is a negated past (نکرد = 'did not do') with ایچ 'nothing/not at all' giving 'did nothing bad at all.' بر شاه یاد is a prepositional phrase 'upon the king's memory/reputation' (بر = on/against, یاد = trace/mark of memory), so the whole clause means he did nothing to blemish the king's repute.
segments.s649-g259: جهاندار is the subject 'the sovereign.' ز او ماند is a short passive/impersonal sequence meaning 'was left/was made remain because of him' (ز = 'from/because of', او = 'him', ماند = past 'remained/was left'). اندر شگفت is a locative phrase 'in wonder/astonishment.' The structure treats the sovereign as the experiencer left amazed by the man's behaviour.
segments.s649-g260: ز کردار او = 'because of his deeds' (ز = 'from/because of', کردار = 'deed/conduct', او = 'his'). مردمی برگرفت uses برگرفت (lit. 'took up/took') transitively with مردمی 'the people' to indicate he 'took/won the people'—i.e., he secured popular support; the verb is past simple and the phrase is a compact poetic way to say 'won over the people.'
segments.s649-g261: همی‌کرد پوزش is a habitual/past progressive construction 'he would apologize' or 'he used to beg pardon.' که introduces the content clause ('that ...'); بدخواه من is the noun phrase 'my ill-wisher' = 'my enemy.' The clause functions as the reported excuse/reason given when begging pardon.
segments.s649-g262: پرآشوب کرد = 'caused turmoil' (پرآشوب = 'tumultuous/causing disturbance', کرد = past 'did/caused'). اختر و ماه من = 'my star and my moon,' a poetic pair for beloveds/fortunate signs; they are the objects of کرد. The verb is causative in sense: 'my enemy stirred up/disturbed my star and moon.'
segments.s649-g263: گر introduces a conditional 'if.' ایدون is an archaic/adverbial word meaning 'now' or 'at this time' (classical usage); که + یابم forms a subjunctive/complement clause 'that I may find' (یابم is subjunctive/present 'I may find/attain'). رهایی ز بند = 'release from the bond' (رهایی = freedom/release, ز بند = from the fetter/bond). The sequence expresses a conditional desire/hope for release.
segments.s649-g264: تو را is the dative/topic-marked 'to you' (تو + را); باشد is an optative/subjunctive form here meaning 'may it be / it will be' (expressing prospective benefit); از هر بدی = 'from/against every harm' (از = 'from/against', هر = 'every', بدی = 'harm/evil'); سودمند is the predicate adjective 'beneficial/useful.' The clause states the hoped-for outcome: your benefit against all harm.
segments.s649-g113: Word order is literary/poetic: indirect object phrase 'بر او' (to him) comes first, followed by the past-tense verb 'خواند' (read) and then the direct object 'آن نامه' (that letter); the subject 'کیقباد' appears last (common in epic Persian). 'بر' here functions as 'to/toward' with the enclitic pronoun 'او' marking the indirect object.
segments.s649-g114: The verb 'بخندید' is past tense (3rd person singular) meaning 'laughed'; the subject 'شاپور مهرک‌نژاد' follows the verb, another example of poetic inversion where verb precedes full-name subject. There is no object; this is an intransitive action.
segments.s649-g115: 'که' introduces the clause ('that'/'who'); 'جز' means 'except' and selects 'سوفزا' as the sole exception; 'دشمن' (enemy) is the head noun, and 'اندر جهان' ('in the world') is a locative phrase modifying it. The main copula/negation follows in the next segment ('ورا نیست').
segments.s649-g116: 'ورا' is a dative/benefactive pronoun meaning 'for him' (or 'to him'); 'نیست' is the present/past-stative copula 'is not/does not exist'; 'در آشکار و نهان' is a paired adverbial phrase 'in open and hidden' = 'neither openly nor secretly'. The clause thus denies the existence of any enemy for him except the one named earlier.
segments.s649-g117: The preposition 'ز' (from) begins the phrase 'ز هر جای' ('from every place'); 'فرمانبران' is the definite/particular group 'the obedient/submissive ones'; the object marker 'را' marks them as the direct object, and 'بخواند' (3rd person past) means 'he called/summoned' — here the sense is 'to summon/call'.
segments.s649-g118: 'سوی' indicates direction 'toward'; 'طیسفون' (Ctesiphon) is the place toward which they move; 'تیز' (here before the noun by poetic inversion) modifies 'لشکر' as 'swift/keen' (i.e., a fast-moving army); 'براند' is a 3rd-person form of راندن meaning 'he/they drove/led' — in epic narrative it functions as simple past 'he sent/led'.
segments.s649-g119: 'چو' here is the temporal conjunction 'when'; 'آورد' is the past tense 'brought' (here best understood as 'came' / 'was brought') with 'لشکر' as its object; 'به نزدیک شاه' is a prepositional phrase 'to/near the king' indicating proximity in relation to the action.
segments.s649-g120: 'هم' here conveys simultaneity ('at the same time' / 'together'); 'اندر زمان' literally 'in the time' = 'at that moment'; 'برگشادند' is plural past 'they opened' (verb before object by poetic word order); 'راه' is the object 'the way/road' that was opened.
segments.s649-g41: همه سر به سر is an adverbial idiom meaning 'one and all' or 'all together' (an emphatic plural). دست نیکی is a noun phrase 'the hand/act of goodness' (دست as a metaphor for action); برید is a past-tense verb here meaning 'ceased/cut off' (an older/poetic sense: to sever or stop). Word order puts the adverbial phrase up front, then the object, then the verb in past tense.
segments.s649-g42: The doubled جهان (repetition for emphasis) marks the scope 'the whole world'; را is the definite/object marker attaching to the second جهان; به بد is a prepositional phrase 'into/unto evil'; مسپرید (past) means 'they scattered/strewn' or 'cast' here, so the whole clause means 'they cast the world into evil'.
segments.s649-g43: همه marks plurality ('all'); مهتران is the plural of a title 'chiefs/elders' (used as the subject). آفرین خواندند is a fixed expression meaning 'they cried out “bravo”/gave acclamation'—خواندند is past plural of خواندن used idiomatically as 'utter/exclaim'. The verb follows the subject in normal past narrative order.
segments.s649-g44: زبرجد is an ablative/instrumental noun 'from peridot' or 'with peridot gems'; به + تاجش marks the goal/recipient 'onto his crown' (تاجش = his crown); برافشاندند is past plural 'they showered/sprinkled' (the verb is causative/putting action onto the crown). Word order places the source first, then the phrase indicating the thing acted upon, then the verb.
segments.s649-g45: جوان بود is a simple past copular phrase 'he was young' (بود = past of 'to be'). سالش 'his age' places the possessed noun before the numerals; the sequence 'سه پنج و یکی' is a poetic/older-number phrasing (numerals linked by و). The clause therefore states his age in an archaic numeric formula.
segments.s649-g46: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'; شاهی here means 'kingship/royal title'; ورا (archaic form of را + او) means 'to him' or 'for him'; بهره بود 'there was a share' uses بود as an existential/past auxiliary meaning 'had' or 'there was to him'; اندکی modifies and means 'a little'—together: 'he had but a small share of kingship.'
segments.s649-g47: همی‌راند is an imperfective/continuous past form (همی- prefix + past stem) meaning 'was driving/ruling' or 'kept on ruling'; کار جهان is the object 'the affairs of the world' (کار = affairs/work; جهان = world); سوفزای is the personal name placed after the clause as the agent. The verb form and word order are typical of epic/poetic narration.
segments.s649-g48: قباد is the subject (a proper name); اندر ایران is a locative prepositional phrase 'in Iran' (اندر = in/within); نبد is an archaic negative copula 'was not'; کدخدای is the predicate noun 'village headman'—so the clause states that Qobad was not a kadehkudai in Iran.
segments.s649-g209: چنین (an adverb) = 'thus/so' prefaces reported speech; گفت is the verb 'said' (here used in past narrative), پس is an adverb 'then' marking sequence, شاه را uses را as the definite/object marker marking 'the king' as the (indirect) addressee/object, and رهنمون is a noun 'guide' (here functioning as the action of showing the way or as 'he guided'). Word order is typical Persian: reported-speech marker + verb, temporal adverb, then the marked object (شاه را) and the noun rhnmon indicating the guiding/action.
segments.s649-g210: که introduces a reported content clause ('that'); یارند is 3rd‑person plural copula + predicate meaning 'are companions/friends' (یار as predicate noun), با is the preposition 'with', او 'him', همه 'all/everyone', and طیسفون is the place name 'Ctesiphon'. The clause literally reads 'that (they) are friends with him, all Ctesiphon.'
segments.s649-g211: همه is a determiner 'all' that scopes over the coordinated noun phrase لشکر و زیردستان ما ('our army and our subordinates'); ما is the possessive/topic 'our' placed after the coordinated nouns (poetic/archaic order but common in classical Persian).
segments.s649-g212: ز is the poetic form of از meaning 'from'; دهقان here denotes the social class (dihqān/landed gentry); وز in the manuscript likely represents the sequence 'و ز' ('and from') contracted in poetry; در before پرستان is the locative/prepositional element 'in' or a particle introducing the following group, and پرستان are 'devotees/adorers'; ما again marks 'our'. The phrase lists additional groups ('from the dehqāns and from our devotees').
segments.s649-g213: گر = 'if' introduces a conditional clause; او 'he' is the subject; اندر is an older/preclassical form of در/درِ meaning 'in/within'; ایران is the place; بماند is the present/subjunctive/past-form of مانَد meaning 'remains' (here a protasis verb); درست functions adverbially = 'securely/properly/indeed' and modifies بماند.
segments.s649-g214: ز شاهی = 'of/on account of kingship' (ز = 'of/from' + شاهی = 'kingship' or 'royal dignity'); بباید is the modal 'must/ought' (3rd‑person form used impersonally here toward 'you'); تو را uses را as an object/ethical dative marker with 'you'; دست شست is an idiom 'to wash one's hands' (شست past of شستن = 'washed'), meaning to renounce or be rid of (i.e., 'you must wash your hands of kingship/you must give up the throne'). Word order places the idiom at the end for emphasis.
segments.s649-g215: بداندیش is an adjective/noun meaning 'malicious' or 'ill‑minded' used as an epithet; شاه جهان = 'king of the world' (شاه modified by جهان); کشته is a past participle 'killed/slain' that here relates to the verb phrase (i.e., 'who had killed' or 'slain'); به at the end of the clause is a flexible prepositional particle ('to/against/by') used in classical Persian to link clauses and indicate direction or relation—here it carries the clause forward into the next phrase.
segments.s649-g216: سر بخت is a compound where سر 'head' + بخت 'fortune/fate' gives the notion of the 'head/turn of fortune' (i.e., the state/direction of fate); بدخواه 'ill‑wisher' qualifies whose fate is meant; برگشته is a perfect/past participle 'has turned/returned' indicating a change of fortune; the trailing به again is a linking/directional particle common in poetry, here implying 'toward/against' and connecting to the next idea. Together the clause says the ill‑wisher's fortune has turned (against him).
segments.s649-g97: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a subordinate clause. The proper name شاپور رازی (شاپور + رازی, a nisba 'of Ray') is the subject and appears before the verb بیاید; بیاید here is a present/subjunctive form used narratively as a past/episodic 'came/rose', and ز جای is a prepositional phrase 'from his place' following the verb.
segments.s649-g98: به درد دل is a prepositional phrase meaning 'into/with the sorrow of the heart' (به = 'towards/into'); بدکنش is an epithet 'the ill‑doer' qualifying the following name سوفزای, which is the agent causing the pain. Classical Persian often omits an explicit verb here, so the phrase should be read as 'Sufzāy the ill‑doer brought/caused (it) to the heart.'
segments.s649-g99: شنید is a simple past 'he heard' with an implied subject from context; این سخن شاه is the direct object 'this speech/these words of the king'. و links to the next clause, and نیرو گرفت is an idiomatic verb phrase 'took/gained strength' (نیرو 'strength' + گرفت 'took').
segments.s649-g100: هنرها (plural) 'his arts/skills' appears before the verb بشست 'he washed/put aside', a common SOV/order where the object precedes the verb; از دل آهو گرفت is a second coordinated verb clause meaning literally 'he took from the gazelle's heart' — idiomatically 'he won the gazelle's heart' (from = از, دل = 'heart', آهو = 'gazelle').
segments.s649-g101: همانگه is an adverbial 'at that moment'; جهاندیده‌ای is an epithet 'worldly‑wise' (an adjective/nominative modifier) attached to the proper name کیقباد (Kay Qobād), the subject of the following verb.
segments.s649-g102: بفرمود is an honorific past tense 'he commanded/ordered'. تا introduces a purpose/subjunctive clause 'so that/in order that', hence the verb برنشیند is in the subjunctive sense 'may he sit/alight'. چو باد is a comparative 'like the wind' (چو = 'as/like', باد = 'wind'), modifying the manner of sitting/alighting.
segments.s649-g103: به نزدیک is an adverbial phrase 'near/toward' placed before the person; شاپور رازی is the proper name 'Shapur of Ray', and شود is the present/preterital 'became/happened' used narratively to mean 'he drew near' or 'he came close'.
segments.s649-g104: بر آواز is a prepositional phrase 'at the sound/cry of' (بر = 'at/on', آواز = 'call/cry'); نخچیر here denotes the prey/game of the hunt, so 'آواز نخچیر' = 'the cry of the hunt/prey'. و connects the coordinated noun بازی 'the sport/game', and شود here is impersonal/auxiliary 'it happened/the game began.'
segments.s649-g337: This clause places the addressee/name زبردست (Zabardast) with the object/indirect-object marker را immediately before the verb گفت. Classical/poetic Persian often uses X را گفت to mean “said to X.” خندان (smiling) is a participial adjective describing قباد (Qobad), the actual speaker. گفت is simple past (he said) and the sequence names the recipient then the speaker: “Qobad, smiling, said to Zabardast.”
segments.s649-g338: کزین بوم begins with the prepositional/relative element کَ+زِ (here contracted to کزین) meaning “from this,” followed by بوم “land, homeland.” هرگز نگیریم is a negative present/subjunctive-ish form: ن- prefix + گیریم (1st pl.) from گرفتن ‘take/seize’ used here idiomatically with یاد ‘remembrance’ at the end; together it yields “we will never take/receive remembrance” i.e. “we shall never forget/lose memory of it.” Word order is fronted adverbial (کزین بوم) then negative verb then the noun یاد as the thing not taken.
segments.s649-g339: چو خواهی = “when/if you will/wish”: چو functions as a temporal/conditional conjunction and خواهی is 2nd-person present/future (“you want/wish”). فرستمت is a verb with the object-clitic -ت (“I will/may send you” or “I shall send you” in classical narrative usage); سپاه is the complement ‘host/army’. بی‌مر is an adjectival/epithet placed before سپاه; it is archaic/poetic here and treated as ‘without equal/peerless’ (see lexical note). The whole clause therefore means “When you want, I will send you with the (peerless) host.”
segments.s649-g340: This clause qualifies the host: چغانی is a nisba/epithet (here uncertain/obscure in meaning — probably a tribal/place-name or type). Two consecutive که introduce a subordinate clause of purpose or result: که باشد (گاه/optative/be) uses باشد as a present/subjunctive/optative “may be/let it be,” and که یازد uses یازد (classical form) “breaks forth/opens” (here “burst forth”). به گاه = “at the time/at the appointed moment.” Overall: “the Chaghani (host), so that it may break forth at the appointed time.”
segments.s649-g341: چو کردَند = “when they made/did” (چو = when; کردند = 3rd pl. past of کردن). عهد is the direct object ‘oath/pledge/covenant.’ آن دو گردن‌فراز is the agent phrase ‘those two proud-necked ones’ (آن دو = those two; گردن‌فراز an adjective/noun meaning ‘proud’ or ‘high‑necked’). Classical order places the verb earlier and the agent phrase after the object for poetic rhythm.
segments.s649-g342: در گنج زر و درم = prepositional list ‘in the treasure of gold and dirhams’ (the repeated در is stylistic). کرد باز is a compound verbal expression: کرد (past ‘did/made’) + باز (here adverbial ‘again’/‘opened again’), giving the sense ‘opened again’ or ‘restored/opened the treasure anew.’ The sentence thus reports that on their covenant the treasure of gold and dirhams was opened again.
segments.s649-g343: 'به' is the dative preposition 'to/for', marking the recipient; 'شاه جهاندار' is a compounded title 'the king, the world-holder' (the recipient). 'دادش' is the past tense verb 'gave' with the enclitic object pronoun '-ش' ('him/it'), here referring back to the king (a redundant object clitic common in classical/poetic Persian). Word order is verb before its direct object, which appears next as 'رمه' (the thing given).
segments.s649-g344: 'سلیح سواران' is a nominal phrase in which 'سلیح' (lit. 'armament/armed') qualifies 'سواران' — together 'armed horsemen' (the adjective-like word precedes the head in poetic usage). 'و' is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. 'لشکر همه' = 'the army, all of it' where 'همه' emphasizes totality ('the whole host'); the whole phrase names additional items given and completes the direct object introduced earlier ('رمه ...').
segments.s649-g1: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. بر تخت بنشست is a verb phrase (بنشست, past tense 'sat down') with the prepositional phrase بر تخت 'on the throne'. The proper name فرخ قباد (Farrokh Qobad) follows the verb (verb–subject order is a poetic inversion).
segments.s649-g2: کلاه بزرگی به سر برنهاد is a main clause: کلاه بزرگی 'a large hat' (noun + descriptive nominal form after the noun), به سر 'onto the head' (prepositional phrase), and برنهاد (past of نهادن) 'placed/put on' as the transitive past verb. The subject ('he') is understood from context.
segments.s649-g3: سوی طیسفون شد is a motion clause: سوی طیسفون 'toward Ctesiphon' and شد (past of شدن) here functions as 'set out / turned / went' (poetic use of شدن for motion). ز شهر صطخر is a simple from‑phrase 'from the city Istakhr'. Word order is fronted adverbial → verb.
segments.s649-g4: که introduces an explanatory/relative clause 'for/that'. آزادگان را بدو is marked with را to highlight 'the nobles' (آزادگان) and بدو 'to him'. بود is the past copula 'was', and فخر is the predicate 'pride' (i.e. 'the nobles had pride in him'). Order: topic/object (آزادگان را) → indirect object (بدو) → copula → predicate.
segments.s649-g5: چو introduces the temporal clause again ('when'). بر تخت پیروز بنشست = 'sat on the throne victorious' where پیروز is a postpositive adjective 'victorious' modifying the subject; بنشست is the past tense verb. گفت here functions as the main past verb 'said' (introducing the reported clause).
segments.s649-g6: که introduces the reported content 'that / (do) ...'. از من مدارید is an admonition addressed to others: از من 'from me' + مدارید (here an imperative/present meaning 'do not withhold / do not keep back' in plural/polite form). چیزی نهفت = 'anything (چیزی) (be) hidden' — literally 'do not keep anything hidden from me.' The object چیزی follows the verb phrase, and نهفت functions as the notion 'conceal/keep hidden' in this clause.
segments.s649-g7: شما را سوی من گشادست راه: شما را marks the addressees ('for you' / 'to you' with را topicalizing the audience), سوی من 'toward me', راه is the subject 'the road/way', and گشادست is a contracted form of گشاد است 'is wide/open'. Word order is topicalized fronting (you/your → toward me) followed by the predicate 'راه گشادست'.
segments.s649-g8: به‌روز سپید و شبان سیاه is a parallel temporal phrase qualifying when the road is open: به‌روز 'by day / in the daytime' + سپید 'bright/white' and شبان 'nights' + سیاه 'black/dark'. Note شبان is a poetic plural/archaic form for 'nights'. The two halves are joined by و 'and'.
segments.s649-g153: خود is an emphatic reflexive/agentive pronoun 'himself' placed before the conjunction و ('and') to stress the principal actor. نامداران is a plural noun 'the renowned/celebrated ones' and پرخاشجوی is a postpositive adjective 'quarrelsome/aggressive' modifying نامداران (poetic style often omits the linking ezāfe). Together the phrase lists two coordinated subjects: 'he himself and the quarrelsome notables.'
segments.s649-g154: سوی introduces a prepositional phrase 'toward(s)'; شهر شیراز is the object 'the city of Shiraz' with the common noun شهر followed by the proper name شیراز. بنهاد is the past tense (3rd sg) of بنهادن (classical/poetic form of نهادن) 'to put/set'; here it takes روی ('face') as its object in the idiom روی بنهادن 'to set/turn one's face (toward)'. The overall order is subject (previous segment) + prepositional phrase + verb + object (poetic word order).
segments.s649-g155: چو here = 'when' (introducing a temporal clause). آگاه شد is the compound verb 'became aware/heard' (آگاه = aware; شد = became). زآن + سخن + سوفزای functions as a genitive/oblique phrase 'of that speech/report concerning Sofuzāy' (ز here = 'about/from that'), so the whole segment means 'when he became aware of that report about Sofuzāy.'
segments.s649-g156: همانگه is an adverb 'at that moment/immediately.' بیاورد is past tense (3rd sg) of آوردن with the prefix بی‑ 'to bring/bring forth/move'; here it means 'he moved/caused to move.' لشکر is the object 'the army/host.' ز (poetic variant of از) جای = 'from the place/positions' — together: 'at once he moved the army from their place/positions.'
segments.s649-g157: پذیره شدش is a passive/experiential construction: پذیره (reception/being received) + شدش (it happened to him/was done to him) → 'he was received' or 'it befell him' (i.e., he found himself attended). با introduces the accompanying instrument/means 'with' and سپاهی گران is a compound noun phrase 'a mighty/heavy army' (سپاهی = army, گران here = mighty/weighty in a laudatory sense).
segments.s649-g158: گزیده is an adjective 'chosen/elite' that modifies the coordinated nouns سواران و جوشنوران ('horsemen and mail‑wearers/armored warriors'); in classical/poetic Persian the ezāfe linking may be omitted. The phrase names the elite mounted and armored troops that accompanied him.
segments.s649-g159: رسیدند is 3rd plural past 'they reached/arrived.' پس = 'then/afterward.' یک به دیگر is an idiom 'one to the other' = 'toward one another/each other.' فراز here is an adverb/noun 'on high/aloft' indicating that the approach or clash happened at height (e.g., while mounted). The sequence describes two parties approaching each other.
segments.s649-g160: فرود آمدند is 3rd plural past 'they descended/came down' (فرود = descent; آمدند = they came). آن دو گردن‌فراز is a nominal phrase 'those two proud-necked ones' (گردن‌فراز = proud, boastful), serving as the subject of فرود آمدند: 'those two proud ones came down.' The clause closes the scene with the two proud figures descending (from their high positions).
segments.s649-g225: This is a nominal + verbal phrase: خروشی (a cry/clamor) is the subject and برآمد is the past verb 'arose/issued forth'. ز ایران is a prepositional phrase 'from Iran' giving the source of the cry. به درد is another prepositional phrase attached to the clause meaning 'in pain' or 'of sorrow' (poetic use), describing the nature of the cry.
segments.s649-g226: زن و مرد و کودک is a coordinated plural subject ('women and men and children'). همی is the archaic/prosodic auxiliary/adverb marking habitual/continuous action (classical Persian), placed before the verb. مویه کرد uses کرد as the main past verb 'did/made' with مویه (lament) as its object, idiomatically 'they bewailed' or 'they wailed'.
segments.s649-g227: برآشفت is a past-tense, intransitive verb meaning 'became agitated/was stirred'. ایران is the logical subject (here the people/land of Iran). و links to the next clause. برخاست 'rose up' is another past verb. گرد is an adverb 'around/about' that will link with the following words (it pairs with گِردِ هم in the next segment to mean 'together/around one another').
segments.s649-g228: همی again marks the action as ongoing/habitual (classical usage). هر کسی is an indefinite distributive subject 'each person/anyone'. کرد is the past verb 'did/made'. ساز نبرد is a noun phrase where ساز (here) means 'device/gear' or 'implements' and نبرد is 'of battle' — together 'implements/gear of war' (i.e., they armed themselves or prepared war-gear).
segments.s649-g229: همی‌گفت is an iterative/past habitual past verb 'kept saying' or 'would say', introducing reported speech. هرکس که ('whoever that') begins a subordinate clause; تخت قباد is the direct object/phrase 'the throne of Qobad' (تخت = throne; قباد = the name Qobad), and this phrase is syntactically bound to the following conditional clause.
segments.s649-g230: اگر ... شد is a standard conditional: اگر = 'if', شد = past-of‌شدن 'becomes' or 'should become'. سوفزا is an archaic/rare adjective used poetically here (sense uncertain — preserved as a fixed epithet); به ایران marks the target/location 'for/to Iran'. مباد is an optative/negatory particle meaning 'may it not be' (wish that it would not occur). Altogether the clause expresses a wish that, should that quality occur, it not afflict Iran.
segments.s649-g231: سپاهی و شهری is a coordinated subject 'soldiers and townspeople/citizens'. همه شد یکی is a verbless/predicative construction with شد (past of شدن) 'became' and یکی as the predicate 'one' — i.e., 'all became one' (they united). Note classical order: subject — شد — predicate.
segments.s649-g232: نبردند here is a past plural verb from بردن used in the sense 'bore/held/took' (they carried or bore something). نام قباد is the direct object 'the name Qobad' (i.e., they bore the name/title “Qobad”). اندکی is an adverb 'a little/for a short while', modifying the whole clause. Thus: 'they bore the name Qobad briefly.'
segments.s649-g193: کنونم combines the adverb کنون (“now”) with an enclitic -م used in classical Persian to link clauses (roughly “now that”). که introduces reported speech or a content clause. فرمود is 3rd-person past (he said/ordered). بندم is a verbal-nominal phrase here: بند (root “to bind”) plus the enclitic -م “me,” functioning as ‘my being bound’ or ‘bind me’ within reported speech rather than an imperative form. سزاست is a predicative copula meaning “it is deserved/fitting.”
segments.s649-g194: سخنهای is the plural construct of سخن (“words/speech”); the Persian plural marker -ها is fused in poetic orthography. ناسودمندم is an adjective (ناسودمند “unprofitable/useless”) with a final -م that here agrees with or refers back to the speaker (“my useless/unprofitable”). سزاست repeats the predicative formula “it is deserved,” linking back to the preceding clause: ‘my useless words deserve this.’
segments.s649-g195: ز فرمان او is a prepositional phrase “from / by his command.” هیچ‌گونه is an emphatic negative modifier “in no way / not at all.” مگرد is an archaic/poetic verbal form meaning here “do not turn aside / do not depart” (an admonitory negative), so the whole segment reads “do not in any way depart from his command.”
segments.s649-g196: چو here means “as / like” (introducing a comparison) followed by پیرایه دان, where پیرایه is “an ornament” and دان (from دانستن in this classical use) means “to consider / to regard.” بند is the “fetter / bond” and بر پای مرد is the locative phrase “on the man's foot.” Word order is typical classical/poetic order: the simile introduced by چو + verb دان takes the following noun phrase as its object: “consider the fetter on the man's foot as an ornament.”
segments.s649-g197: چو + past verb بنشست = “when he sat down”; in classical Persian the verb can precede the subject, so بنشست شاپور = “when Shapur sat down.” پایش is پای + the possessive enclitic -ش “his (foot).” ببست is the past 3rd-person verb meaning “he bound/tied,” so the clause reads “when Shapur sat down, he bound his foot.”
segments.s649-g198: بزد is the past 3rd-person of زدن (“he struck/hit”). نای رویین is a noun + adjective construction: نای (a reed/pipe, here likely a musical or hollow tube) qualified by رویین (“iron-made / iron‑hard”). و خود برنشست = “and he himself sat upon (it),” where خود is emphatic (“himself”) and برنشست is a compound past verb meaning “sat upon / took a seat on.”
segments.s649-g199: بیاوردش is past 3rd-person of آوردن with the object enclitic -ش (“he brought him”). از پارس indicates the origin/place “from Pars (Persia).” پیش قباد means “in the presence of Qobad” or “before Qobad.” Word order places the verb before its subject/object as common in epic verse, but the enclitic marks the direct object clearly.
segments.s649-g200: This clause reads literally “Qobad, of the past, did not make any memory”: قباد is the subject; از گذشته is a genitival/oblative phrase “of/from the past” (meaning ‘regarding the past’); نکرد is the past negative auxiliary “did not do/make” used here with یاد as its object to mean ‘did not recall’; ایچ is an archaic emphatic negative “nothing/at all”; یاد is “memory/remembering.” Together: “Qobad did not recall anything of the past.”
segments.s649-g385: کنون is an adverb 'now' setting the time. کامرانی is the nominal subject 'good fortune/success'. بدان کت is an adverbial/prepositional phrase here: بدان is an archaic compound (به + آن = 'to/at that' or 'in that') preposing 'کت' (coat); کت هواست is completed by هوا + است (see below). The predicate of the whole small clause is هواست ('is a whim/desire').
segments.s649-g386: که begins a subordinate/explanatory clause ('for/because' or 'that'); شاه جهان is a title phrase 'the king of the world' (شاه = king, جهان = world as genitive attribute). بر جهان is a prepositional phrase 'over/upon the world'. پادشاست is an old fused form meaning 'is sovereign / is king' (پادشاه + است contracted), acting as the clause predicate.
segments.s649-g387: پیاده is an adverb/adjectival phrase 'on foot' modifying the action. همه is the plural subject 'all/everyone'. پیش او = 'in his presence / before him'. در + دوان (a deverbal noun) forms an adverbial expression 'در دوان' = 'while running / in running' (i.e., they rushed). Word order is flexible: adverbs/phrases precede the verb that follows.
segments.s649-g388: برفتند is the past plural verb 'they went / they advanced'. پر + خاک is an adjectival phrase 'full of dust' or 'dust-covered' (پر = 'full (of)'). تیره‌روان is an epithet/adjective 'dark-faced' or 'gloomy/ill-fated' describing their appearance; together these modify the subject and describe how they went.
segments.s649-g389: This clause follows Persian object–verb–subject flexibility: گناه بزرگان is the direct object 'the sins of the elders'. ببخشید is the past transitive verb 'forgave'. شاه (the subject) follows the verb here—so the natural order is 'شاه ... ببخشید گناه بزرگان' = 'the king forgave the elders' sins.'
segments.s649-g390: ز introduces the reason or source ('for/because of'). خون ریختن is a verbal-noun construction 'the shedding/spilling of blood' (ز + [verbal noun]). کرد پوزش is a light-verb construction where کرد ('made/did') combines with the noun پوزش ('apology') to mean 'made an apology' or 'offered pardon'. به راه here idiomatically completes the phrase (roughly 'in [this] manner' or simply as a periphrastic closing), so overall 'because of the bloodshed he made/issued an apology.'
segments.s649-g391: ببخشید is again the past transitive verb 'forgave'. جاماسب را is the direct object with the postposed definite/object marker را attached to the proper name. همچنین is an adverb 'also/likewise', indicating that Jamasp too received forgiveness. The clause order emphasizes the object before the verb and adds the adverbial note.
segments.s649-g392: بزرگان is the subject 'the elders/great ones'. بر او is a prepositional phrase 'to/toward him' indicating the addressee. خواندند here means 'they called out' or 'they uttered' (an action of voicing an acclamation), and آفرین is the acclamation 'Bravo!'—so the elders cried out 'Afarin!' to him.
segments.s649-g393: This clause contains two past-tense verbs in sequence: بیامد (he came) followed by برنشست (he sat upon). The phrase به تخت کیی places the goal/locative after the verb: به = 'to', تخت = 'throne' and کیی (Kayanian) is a postpositive epithet modifying تخت (noun + adjective after noun, a common poetic order). برنشست is the compound verb (preverb بر- + نشست) meaning 'sat upon'.
segments.s649-g394: ورا (to him/his) is an indirect/possessive element placed before the verb گشت (became). The structure ورا گشت + [name + predicate] literally reads 'to him became Jamasp, sycophant' — i.e. Jamasp became his sycophant. گشت is past tense 'became', and جاماسب مهترپرست is a proper name followed by a predicate noun (مهترپرست) describing him.
segments.s649-g395: بر این‌گونه is a manner phrase: بر + این‌گونه = 'in this way' or 'thus'. تا is the subordinating conjunction 'until' that introduces the temporal clause; گشت is past 'became'. The sequence تا گشت کسری بزرگ literally places the verb before the subject (poetic inversion): 'until Kisra became great.'
segments.s649-g396: یکی here is the indefinite 'a certain', followed by کودکی used as 'a (young) child/boy'. شد is past 'became' or 'there was' (introducing the new person). The adjectives دلیر و سترگ follow the predicate, giving qualities of that child; note the second adjective (سترگ) is archaic/poetic.
segments.s649-g397: به فرهنگیان is a prepositional phrase 'to the scholars/men of learning' (recipient), داد is the past-tense verb 'gave/entrusted', and فرزند را is the definite object marked by را. Word order is typical Persian: indirect object (به فرهنگیان) + verb (داد) + object (فرزند را).
segments.s649-g398: چنان introduces a comparative/simile 'such' or 'so'; بار here means 'charge' or 'burden/responsibility' and شاخ برومند is a noun phrase 'a noble/fruitful bough' (شاخ = bough/branch, برومند a descriptive adjective after the noun). The final را is topical/emphatic, linking this simile back to what was entrusted.
segments.s649-g399: همه کار ایران و توران is a topical noun phrase 'all the affairs of Iran and Turan' (همه modifies کار). The verb بساخت is a past tense verb meaning 'made/arranged/set in order' (poetic diction); note the poetic ordering places the verb after the topic phrase here.
segments.s649-g400: This clause describes a grand image: کلاه مهی 'a moon-like/celestial crown' (کلاه = hat/crown, مهی = moon-like) and برفراخت is past 'raised/erected' or 'displayed'. The initial بگردون reflects an older/poetic prepositional/locative element (in sense of 'to the sphere/heavens' or related verbal prefix) tied to placing the crown toward the sky; the exact choice of the lemma's supplied senses does not match this poetic usage.
segments.s649-g201: بفرمود is the honorific/past third-person verb 'he commanded/said'. The clause کو را uses the archaic interrogative/relative کو with the object marker را, functioning here as 'who/whom' (referring back to someone previously mentioned) and is the object of برند. به زندان is a prepositional phrase ('into/ to prison') where به takes the sense 'towards/into'. برند is the subjunctive/jussive (classical present) form of بردن used after a command verb to express 'they should take' or narrative 'they took'.
segments.s649-g202: به نزدیک is another prepositional phrase: به ('toward/into') + نزدیک ('near'); ناهوشمندان is the plural noun 'the unwise (ones)' and functions as the locative target of برند. The verb برند is repeated here (parallel to the previous clause) and again expresses the action 'take/bring' in a jussive/narrative sense.
segments.s649-g203: به شیراز فرمود places an indirect-location/recipient with فرمود: 'he commanded to/at Shiraz' (به as indirect object marker 'to'). تا introduces a content clause ('that/so that') and هرچه بود is the subordinate nominal clause meaning 'whatever there was' (هرچه 'whatever' + بود 'was' in the past).
segments.s649-g204: This is a coordinated list specifying the contents of 'هرچه': ز here is the poetic preposition 'of/from' and links each noun (مردان 'men', گنج 'treasure', کشت 'crops' (i.e. cultivated produce)) with و 'and'. درود stands as a final item (here best read as 'gifts/offerings' or 'blessings' in a material sense). The whole phrase completes 'هرچه بود'.
segments.s649-g205: بیاورد here functions as a past/narrative verb 'they brought' (classical Persian uses this form in narrative). یک سر is the direct object 'one head' (سر = head), and سوی طیسفون is a directional phrase 'toward Ctesiphon' (سوی = toward, طیسفون = Ctesiphon). Word order is verb–object–direction.
segments.s649-g206: سپردش is the past verb 'he entrusted/handed it over' with the enclitic object -ش ('it' or 'him'). به گنجور marks the indirect object 'to the treasury/treasury-house' (به = to). او رهنمون is appositive/identifying: او ('he') with رهنمون ('guide'), i.e. 'to him, the guide' — linking the entrusted object to that person. The clause thus expresses 'he entrusted it to the treasury, to his guide.'
segments.s649-g207: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; چو یک هفته بگذشت means 'when a week passed' (بگذشت is past 'passed'). هرگونه رای is a noun phrase 'every kind/sort of counsel/plan' and serves as the object of the following verb.
segments.s649-g208: همی‌راند is an imperfective/habitual-form (the prefix همی- marks ongoing or repeated action) meaning 'he was driving/pursuing' or 'he kept pressing (plans/counsel)'. با موبد از سوفزای attaches an instrumental/associative phrase 'with the mobed Sofzā' — با = 'with', موبد = 'mōbed (Zoroastrian priest)', and از before the proper name here indicates 'named/called' or 'of' (classical usage often uses از + name to identify someone).
segments.s649-g81: همه پارس چون بنده او شدند is a temporal/conditional clause: "چون" here functions as a conjunction meaning "when/after" (classical use). The word order is emphatic (همه پارس = 'all the people of Pars'), followed by the predicate بنده او شدند (بنده = 'servant', او = 'him', شدند = past plural of شدن 'become'), so literally 'when all Pars became his servants.' The plural verb شدند agrees with the collective subject همه پارس.
segments.s649-g82: This is a nominal predicate with the copula: بزرگان (the great ones, subject) + پرستنده (a present-participle/agent noun 'worshipper/devotee' used as predicate) + او شدند (they became his). شد(ند) is the past plural of شدن used as a linking verb.
segments.s649-g83: ز here is the poetic preposition 'from/because of'. The clause follows an SOV-like order common in classical Persian: ز گفتار بد (from/because of bad speech) + شد (became/was made) + دل کیقباد (Kay Qobad's heart). شد is the past/inchoative 'became/was affected', so 'Kay Qobad's heart was affected by harsh words.'
segments.s649-g84: ز رنجش is a causal phrase 'out of/for resentment'. به دل برنکرد is a negative perfective: برنکرد ('did not do / refrained from doing') with به دل marking the target 'into the heart'. ایچ یاد = 'any/no memory at all' (ایچ = nothing, emphatic). Together: 'out of resentment he did not put any remembrance into his heart.'
segments.s649-g85: همی‌گفت‌گر is an archaic/conventional speech-reporting and conditional framing: همی‌گفت ('he used to say') + گر (if). The clause that follows is a conditional introduced by گر: من فرستم سپاه — فرستم is the present-subjunctive/optative form used in classical Persian for the protasis of a conditional ('if I send'), and سپاه is the direct object 'an army/host.'
segments.s649-g86: سر او بگردد is literal 'his head turns' with بگردد in the subjunctive/potential (would/should turn) after the conditional; او is the possessor. شود is the copular verb 'become' and links to the predicate رزمخواه ('war-desirer' / 'one eager for battle') used as a predicate noun/adjective. The sequence gives two consequences: 'his head will turn' (i.e. his attention/resolve will change) and 'he will become eager for battle.'
segments.s649-g87: چو here functions as a conditional 'if'. کرده باشم is the perfect-subjunctive/perfect tense ('have made / should have made') used in the protasis of a condition. من (I) is the subject, دشمنی کرده باشم = 'if I have done enmity / if I have made him an enemy', and به گنج marks the matter/reason ('over the treasure').
segments.s649-g88: از او دید باید is a modal/result clause: از او ('from him') + دید (here 'experience/receive' in the sense of 'suffer/meet with') + باید ('one must / it is to be expected'). بسی is an adverbial intensifier 'much / great' (classical usage), followed by the nouns درد و رنج 'pain and suffering'. Together: 'one must expect from him much pain and suffering.'
segments.s649-g217: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a subordinate clause. بشنید is a simple past (3rd sg.) 'he heard' placed before its subject (poetic word order). مهتر is the subject 'the chief/elder' and ز موبد is a prepositional phrase 'from the mobad' specifying the source of the speech; سخن is the object 'speech/words'.
segments.s649-g218: بنو is a proper-name/clan label used as the subject; تاخت is a past verb 'rushed/charged'. و links the two predicates. بیزار شد is a light-verb construction where بیزار (adjective 'averse/disgusted') takes شد (become) to form 'became averse'. از کهن is a prepositional phrase 'from/of the old' (i.e. old ways/old man) modifying the adjective.
segments.s649-g219: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past 'he said/commanded'. پس is temporal 'then'. تاش is a noun 'effort/attempt' used with the verb کنند to mean 'that they attempt/strive'. بیجان is an adjective 'lifeless' functioning as the result/object of the attempt; کنند is 3rd pl. present/subjunctive 'they do' used here after an imperative/command to express 'let them do/that they do'.
segments.s649-g220: The phrase begins with repeated بر (on/upon) for emphasis: بر او 'upon him' then بر دل و دیده 'upon (his) heart and eye'. پیچان is a present-stem/participle meaning 'to wind/twist/wrap' and together with کنند (they do) forms a subjunctive/imperative clause 'that they wrap/twist (it) upon his heart and eyes'. Grammatically, the prepositional phrases specify the target of the action demanded by the previous clause.
segments.s649-g221: بکردند is a past plural 'they did/caused'. پس is 'then'. پهلوان را uses the object marker را after the noun to mark it as the patient/topic of the action. تباه is an adjective 'ruined/destroyed' used predicatively; the normal order in colloquial Persian would be پهلوان را تباه کردند, but here the verb-front order (بکردند ... تباه) reflects older/poetic syntax.
segments.s649-g222: شد is the past copula 'became/was'. آن is a demonstrative 'that/the'. گرد here is functioning as a poetic/archaic noun referring to the person described (the available sense entries do not include this exact poetic meaning). فرزانه و نیک‌خواه are adjectival modifiers 'wise and well-wishing/benevolent' that qualify that person. The whole phrase is a short predicative remark following the statement of ruin.
segments.s649-g223: چو is 'when' introducing the clause. آگاهی is the noun 'information/news' and آمد is the past 'came' (the verb precedes its topic here, a common poetic order). به ایرانیان is a dative prepositional phrase 'to the Iranians' marking who received the news.
segments.s649-g224: که is the complementizer 'that' introducing the reported clause. آن پیلتن را uses آن ('that') plus the name/place پیلتن with را as a topical object marker. سرآمد زمان is a nominal predicate 'the foremost/eminence of the time' (سرآمد 'foremost' + زمان 'time/age'), so the clause reports that Piltan was the foremost of his age.
segments.s649-g89: کند is the finite verb here (3rd-sg. classical present of کردن) used with یاد to form ‘remember’ (کند + یاد = ‘remembers’). هر کسی is the indefinite subject ‘whoever/anyone’, and کردار اوی is a genitive phrase ‘the deeds of him’ (اوی is the possessive ‘his’ placed after کردار). Word order is classical: verb then subject phrase then object phrase.
segments.s649-g90: نهانی is an adjective/adverb ‘hidden/secret’; ندانند is 3rd-person plural present negative of دانستن ‘do not know/are unaware’; بازار اوی is ‘his market/dealings’ with اوی as possessive. The clause means literally ‘they do not know his secret market/dealings.’
segments.s649-g91: ندارم is the first-person singular present negative of داشتن ‘I do not have’. ز ایران is the poetic prepositional phrase ‘from Iran’. یکی رزمخواه is the indefinite object ‘a single/one fighter’ (یکی = one/a certain, رزمخواه = an aspiring fighter/war-desirer). Together: ‘I do not have one fighter from Iran.’
segments.s649-g92: کز is the relative/connector ‘who/that/from which’ introducing a subordinate clause. ایدر = ‘from here’; شود is the classical 3rd-sg. present/optative of شدن often rendered ‘would become/would go’ here meaning ‘would come/advance’; پیش او = ‘before/in front of him’; با سپاه = ‘with an army’. The clause thus describes the (nonexistent) fighter: ‘who from here would come before him with an army.’
segments.s649-g93: بدو is the dative ‘to him’; گفت is the past tense ‘said’ (classical phrasing ‘he said to him’). فرزانه is the speaker’s epithet ‘the wise one’. مندیش is an archaic/poetic imperative ‘think/consider!’ (used here as an instruction). ز این = ‘about this/of this’ introduces what to consider.
segments.s649-g94: که introduces the content clause ‘that …’. او is ‘he’; شهریاری is the noun ‘sovereignty/kingship’ (object of شود); شود is 3rd-sg. present/optative of شدن ‘may become’ (expressing potential/result); بآفرین is an interjection/adverb meaning literally ‘with praise’ or an exclamation like ‘bravo’ appended to the clause. The clause reads ‘that he may become a king — with praise.’
segments.s649-g95: تو را is the classical dative/possessive construction ‘to you’ (used to indicate possession or that something belongs to you). بندگانند is 3rd-plural ‘are servants’ — i.e. ‘you have servants’. و سالار هست joins another existential clause ‘and a lord exists/there is a chief’. The clause structure is classical/poetic word order.
segments.s649-g96: که is the relative ‘who/that’ referring back to the plural reference (servants and lord collectively). سایند is 3rd-person plural present of ساییدن ‘they rub/they press/fret’; بر = ‘on/upon’; چرخ گردنده = ‘the rotating wheel’ (گردنده = turning/rotating qualifies چرخ ‘wheel’ — here a metaphorical wheel of fortune); دست = ‘hand’. The clause literally says ‘they rub/lay hands upon the turning wheel.’
segments.s649-g73: This correlative negation uses نه ... نه ... to say 'neither ... nor ...'. فرمانش is the possessed noun ('his command') with the possessive enclitic -ش. باشد is a poetic/present-subjunctive form of بودن ('to be') used here to express that his command 'was' or 'had effect' (literally 'be'), and به چیزی means 'in/over anything' (به + چیزی). رای follows as the second item of the correlative negation ('nor his counsel/opinion').
segments.s649-g74: جهان شد is a simple past/resultative 'became' (جهان = 'the world', شد = 'became'). The predicate is همه بنده سوفزای: همه ('all/everyone') + بنده ('servants') + سوفزای (a proper name). Word order is topical: 'the world became — all servants of Sofzay.'
segments.s649-g75: هر آن کس که is a fixed relative phrase meaning 'whoever' or 'anyone who' (هر = 'every/any', آن کس = 'that person'). بد modifies رازدار (adjective before noun, 'bad/confidant'), and قباد is the name of the person possessed (i.e. 'Qobad's').
segments.s649-g76: This phrase contains two occurrences of the preposition بر. بر او here functions 'against/toward him' and the second بر before سخنها is 'on/about' (literary overlap in uses). همی‌کرد is the habitual/continuous past auxiliary 'would do / used to do' (poetic imperfective), and یاد following کرد forms the verbal idea 'to mention/bring to mind' (کرد یاد = 'would recall/utter as a reminder').
segments.s649-g77: که introduces the reported content ('that...'). از پادشاهی 'from/for kingship' (از = 'from/of'), به نامی is a literary use of به + نامی meaning 'by/with a name' or 'merely in name', and بسند is an archaic/poetic present meaning 'it suffices / is enough' (here impersonal/neutral sense 'is enough').
segments.s649-g78: چرا is the interrogative 'why'. کردی is the simple past 2nd-person singular 'you did'. The enclitic -ای attached here (کردی‌ای) is an archaic vocative/exclamatory particle addressing the addressee, followed by شهریار بلند 'O lofty king' (شهریار = 'king', بلند = 'lofty' used adjectivally).
segments.s649-g79: ز is the poetic form of 'from/of' introducing a comparison or source ('from your treasure'). گنج تو 'your treasure' (گنج + possessive تو). آگنده‌تر is the comparative adjective 'more filled / fuller'. The clause ends with گنج او 'his treasure' (repeated noun for emphasis): literally 'from your treasure, his treasure is fuller.'
segments.s649-g80: بباید is the modal/obligative 'must/ought' (present/future sense in poetic register). گسست here functions as a nominal or verbal infinitival notion 'separation/being broken off' (poetic passive sense 'must be separated'). از جهان marks the source/scene ('from the world') and رنج او 'his suffering/anguish' is the thing to be separated. The whole reads 'his suffering must be separated from the world.'
segments.s649-g25: چنان هم که باید is a fixed concessive/affirmative phrase meaning “just as it should be” (چنان = “so/thus”; هم intensifies). که introduces the subordinate clause and باید is the impersonal/modal “ought/should.” دل لشکری is a noun phrase where لشکری (military, “of the army”) modifies دل (“heart”) — the ezāfe is omitted in poetry, so read as دلِ لشکری (“the army’s heart”).
segments.s649-g26: همه is the object/topic “everyone.” در نکوهش is an adverbial/prepositional phrase “in reproach” (در + noun; the ezāfe is omitted poetically). کند is the present/simple verb “does/makes” (here ‘reproaches’), and کهتری is used as a predicative/object meaning “lesser/inferior” (i.e. ‘calls [them] inferior’). The expected pronoun object is omitted (typical in poetry).
segments.s649-g27: توانگر کجا سخت باشد به چیز is a rhetorical question: توانگر “the wealthy one”; کجا here functions as ‘how/where (could)’ introducing the question; سخت means “harsh/severe”; باشد (third-person subjunctive/optative of بودن) expresses possibility/ability (‘could be’); به چیز means “about/with respect to the thing” — the phrase asks how a rich man could be severe in this matter.
segments.s649-g28: فرومایه‌تر is the comparative “more base/baser” (the -تر comparative). شد is the past/simple verb “became.” ز in classical/poetic usage can serve as the comparative marker “than” (here = “than the dervish”). درویش is the reference point (dervish/poor man), and نیز adds ‘also/even.’ Together: ‘he becomes even baser than the dervish.’
segments.s649-g29: چو is the subordinating conjunction “when/if.” درویش نادان is a noun + adjective: دَرویش “dervish” and نادان “ignorant/foolish.” کند is the verbal auxiliary “does/makes” used with مهتری (here ‘to assume superiority’): کند مهتری = ‘acts superior / makes himself chief.’
segments.s649-g30: به + noun + ماند is an idiom meaning ‘amounts to / is equivalent to.’ دیوانگی = “madness”; ماند (third-person singular) = “remains / amounts.” این داوری = “this judgment” (این demonstrative before noun). So the clause reads ‘this judgment amounts to madness.’
segments.s649-g31: چو = ‘when/if.’ عیب تن خویش is a possessive noun phrase ‘the fault(s) of his own person’ — note the poetic split of خویش into خوی + ش (خوی = ‘disposition/self’, ش = possessive ‘his’). داند is the verb ‘knows’ (third-person singular). کسی (‘someone’) is the indefinite subject; word order places the verb after the possessed noun phrase, which is common in classical Persian.
segments.s649-g32: ز عیب کسان = ‘with regard to / concerning the faults of others’ (ز used poetically for ‘concerning/than’). برنخواند is a negative verb form meaning ‘will not call out / will not recite/bring up’ (the prefix بر- with خواندن and negative sense); بسی is an adverb ‘much/often’ or ‘to a great extent,’ modifying the verb — together: ‘he will not often/for the most part call attention to others’ faults.’
segments.s649-g9: بزرگ آن کسی کو is a classical word order meaning 'great is that person who...'; آن کسی کو functions as a relative phrase ('that person who'), with کو used as an older relative marker. به گفتار راست is an adverbial/prepositional phrase ('in/with right speech') that modifies کسی, where به introduces the manner/medium and راست qualifies گفتار.
segments.s649-g10: زبان را is the definite/explicit object (را marks the definite object). بیاراست is a transitive verb ('adorns/sets straight') agreeing with the omitted subject کسی; و links the coordinate clause. کژی نخواست is a negated past/present classical form ('did not want/desire crookedness'), with the same subject carried over from the previous clause.
segments.s649-g11: چو introduces a temporal/conditional clause ('when'/'if'). بخشایش is the direct object of آرد; آرد is third-person singular classical present/past form 'brings'. به خشم اندرون is a directional/adverbial phrase ('into inward anger' / 'to the inner anger'), with به indicating direction/placement.
segments.s649-g12: سر راستان is a noun phrase 'the head/chief of the righteous' (سر = chief/head, راستان = upright/righteous). خواندش is a past tense verb with enclitic object -ش ('he called him' or 'he named him'), and رهنمون is the predicate/complement ('guide') naming what he called him.
segments.s649-g13: نهد is here a verbal form 'sets/places' (classical third-person). تخت خشنودی is the direct object 'the throne of pleasure/contentment' (تخت = throne, خشنودی = pleasure/approval). اندر جهان is a locative adverbial 'in the world'.
segments.s649-g14: بیابد is third-person present 'he finds/obtains'. به داد is an instrumental phrase 'by/through justice' (به used adverbially = 'by'). آفرین مهان is the thing obtained, 'the acclamation/praise of the guests' (آفرین = acclamation, مهان = guests); word order places the instrument before the object.
segments.s649-g15: دل خویش را is a possessed definite object 'his own heart' (خوی + ـش = 'his disposition'), with را marking the object. دور دارد ز کین is a verb phrase meaning 'keeps (it) far from hatred' (دور دارد = 'keeps at a distance', ز = 'from', کین = 'hatred/vengeance').
segments.s649-g16: مهان و کهانش is a coordinated subject 'guests and the ancients/elders' (کهان here in the older/poetic sense 'ancients' or 'elders'); کنند is third-person plural present 'they make/do' and governs آفرین as the object 'acclamation'—i.e., 'they praise (him).' The clitic -ش on کهانش is a possessive/object marker linking back to the praised person.
segments.s649-g169: تو را (object pronoun + object marker) precedes the verb for emphasis: ‘you’ as definite object. بند فرمود is a light‑verb construction: بند (fetter/bond) + فرمود (past tense of فرمودن) = ‘ordered (you) bound’ or ‘had you fettered.’ شاه جهان is the agent/title placed after the verb for poetic emphasis: ‘the sovereign (king) of the world’ (literally ‘king—world’).
segments.s649-g170: فراوان is an adverb modifying the verb (here: ‘greatly/much’). بنالید is a past tense lament verb (third person, plural/indefinite) — the subject is omitted and understood from context (‘they/people bewailed’). پیش مهان is a prepositional phrase meaning ‘in the presence of / before the guests/nobles,’ with پیش as the preposition and مهان the affected group.
segments.s649-g171: بر آن سان که is a fixed phrase meaning ‘in the way/manner that.’ برخوانده‌ای is a perfect (2nd person) form ‘you have read/summoned aloud’ (here: ‘you have read [the letter]’); نامه را is the definite object with را after the noun, marking ‘the letter’ as definite. The clause as a whole = ‘as you have read the letter.’
segments.s649-g172: تو دانی is present tense, 2nd person singular ‘you know.’ شهنشاه خودکامه را is the object phrase: شهنشاه ‘emperor / king of kings’ modified by خودکامه ‘self‑willed, autocratic’; را marks the phrase as definite. Word order places the object after the verb for emphasis, common in poetic diction.
segments.s649-g173: چنین داد پاسخ is ‘gave such an answer / thus gave an answer,’ with چنین preceding the verb phrase to mark manner. بدو is a dative clitic/phrase ‘to him,’ and پهلوان (here placed after the verb phrase) is the agent ‘the champion/hero’ — subject appearing post‑verb for poetic word order.
segments.s649-g174: که introduces a content clause ‘that…’. داند is present tense third‑person ‘(he) knows.’ مرا (written here split as م + را) is the direct object ‘me,’ fronted before the subject for emphasis. شهریار جهان is the subject phrase ‘the sovereign / lord of the world’ (placed after the object), so the clause reads ‘that the sovereign of the world knows me.’
segments.s649-g175: بدان here is a fused prepositional expression (به + آن → بدان) meaning ‘by/with/for that / because of that’; it heads the phrase ‘بدان رنج و سختی’ = ‘because of that suffering and hardship.’ که introduces the relative / explanatory clause ‘that I bore/suffered,’ and بردم is first‑person past ‘I bore/experienced.’ ز شاه uses poetic ز (instead of از) meaning ‘from / at the hands of the shah.’
segments.s649-g176: برفتم is first‑person past ‘I went away / I departed.’ ز زابلستان is the origin phrase ‘from Zabulistan’ (ز = poetic 'from'). با سپاه is an accompanying/instrumental phrase ‘with an army,’ with با meaning ‘with’ and سپاه ‘army/host.’ Together: ‘I departed from Zabulistan with an army.’
segments.s649-g233: برفتند is the past 3rd-person plural of بر‌رفتن (prefix بر + رفتن), ‘they went’ or ‘they departed’. یکسر is an adverb ‘all at once/straightaway’ modifying the verb. به ایوان شاه is a prepositional phrase ‘to the king’s iwan/portico’ (poetic word order often omits the ezafe between ایوان and شاه).
segments.s649-g234: ز here introduces the reason or motive (‘because of/from’ in poetic register). The following three nouns (بدگوی، پردرد، فریادخواه) form a triad describing people who brought the matter: ‘the slanderer’, ‘the one full of pain/sorrow’, and ‘the one crying for help’. Adjectival/nominal compounds like پردرد appear postpositively without extra particles in poetry.
segments.s649-g235: کسی را که ... بود is a common relative/indefinite construction meaning ‘anyone who ... was’. را marks the person as definite/accusative/emphatic. بر + شاه indicates direction/target ‘against the king’. بدگوی is predicate-nominal/adjective ‘one who speaks ill of’ and بود is the past copula ‘was’.
segments.s649-g236: بداندیش او و بلاجوی بود contains two coordinated predicate nouns/adjectives (‘ill‑wishing toward him’ and ‘one who seeks calamity’) joined by و. او is the pronominal object of بداندیش; a single بود (copula) at the end governs both predicates, a typical Persian pattern where the verb follows coordinated predicates.
segments.s649-g237: بکشتند is past 3rd‑person plural ‘they killed’. The verbs are joined by و. بردند is another past 3rd‑person plural ‘they carried/took’ and is followed by the prepositional phrase ز ایوان ‘from the iwan’; کشان is a participial/adverbial form ‘dragging’ that describes the manner (‘dragging them off’). Objects are omitted because they are understood from context.
segments.s649-g238: ز جاماسب جستند uses جستند (past of جستن) in the sense ‘they sought/asked’, not ‘leapt’; the preposition ز marks source/whom they asked (‘from Jamasp’). چندی نشان places a quantity/temporal phrase ‘for a while / some sign’ after the verb: they sought a sign for some time. Word order (verb then object phrase) is typical of the epic register.
segments.s649-g239: که here introduces a reported clause (‘that’). کهتر برادر literally ‘the lesser/inferior brother’ (often used for ‘younger brother’ or one of lower rank). بد و سرفراز are two coordinating adjectives ‘bad/evil and proud/victorious’ modifying برادر; Persian poetry commonly omits a copula in such descriptive phrases.
segments.s649-g240: قبادش is Qobad with the clitic possessive -ش (‘his Qobad’ or ‘Qobad, his’ depending on reference) and functions as the object. همی‌پروریدی is 2nd‑person singular imperfect/past‑habitual ‘you were nurturing/you used to rear’ (همی marks ongoing/habitual action in classical Persian). به ناز is a manner phrase ‘with indulgent fondness’ (به introduces the manner and ناز = pampering/affection).
segments.s649-g57: This is a nominal phrase + verb: 'سپهبد خود و لشکرش' = 'the spahbed himself and his army' where 'خود' is emphatic 'himself' and 'لشکرش' has the possessive clitic '-ش' = 'his army'. 'ساز کرد' uses 'ساز' (here 'means/preparations') as a noun and 'کرد' (past tense 'made/did') to mean 'made preparations' or 'got ready'. Word order is subject then verbal predicate.
segments.s649-g58: 'بزد کوس' is literal 'he struck/sounded the drum' (بزد = past of ضرب/زدن). The construction 'آهنگ ... کرد' means 'made his intent/direction to ...' (i.e. 'set his course for'); here 'آهنگ شیراز کرد' = 'he directed himself toward Shiraz'. 'کرد' is the past auxiliary completing 'آهنگ کردن'.
segments.s649-g59: 'همی‌رفت' is an imperfective/continuous past form (the habitual/continuous past marked by همی-), so 'he was going'/'he went on'. 'شادان' is a predicative/adverbial adjective 'joyful'. 'سوی شهر خویش' is a prepositional phrase 'toward his city' with 'خویش' realized as 'خوی' + enclitic '-ش' = 'his own'.
segments.s649-g60: This is a participial/adverbial clause modifying the subject: 'ز هر کام برداشته' = 'having taken/from every desire' where 'ز' = 'from', 'هر کام' = 'every wish', and 'برداشته' is a past participle (or perfect form) meaning 'taken/fulfilled'. 'بهر خویش' = 'for himself' ('بهر' = 'for/toward', 'خویش' = his own). Together: 'having satisfied every desire for himself.'
segments.s649-g61: 'همه پارس او را شده چون رهی' literal order: 'all Pars' (subject) + 'او را' (to/for him, with object/beneficiary marking) + 'شده' (has become) + 'چون رهی' ('as a road' / 'like a path'). The clause means 'the whole of Pars had become to him like a road' (i.e., lay open to him). Note 'چون' = 'like/as' and 'رهی' functions as 'road/way' in this simile.
segments.s649-g62: 'همی‌بود' is the same imperfective/continuous past 'he continued to be'. 'با تاج شاهنشهی' is an instrumental/associative phrase 'with the imperial crown' (با = with, تاج = crown, شاهنشهی = imperial/kingship). The whole segment states that he continued in his imperial status.
segments.s649-g63: The first tokens are archaic/contracted: 'ب' here functions as the verbal prefix (be-), plus 'دان' ('to know') giving 'بدان' — an imperative or hortative 'know/let it be known'. 'من شاه بنشاندم' is straightforward: 'I' (من) + 'شاه' (king, object) + 'بنشاندم' (I seated/placed/established — past of بنشانیدن = 'to seat/enthrone').
segments.s649-g64: 'به شاهی' = 'to/for kingship' (here 'in kingship' or 'to kingship'), 'بر او' = 'over him' (literally 'upon him'), and 'آفرین خواندم' is an idiom meaning 'I acclaimed/praised' (آفرین = acclamation, خواندم = I uttered/said). The whole means 'I acclaimed him as king' or 'I pronounced praise over him for kingship.'
segments.s649-g121: 'چو' is a temporal conjunction meaning 'when'. 'دیدش' is past tense 'saw' with the enclitic object '-ش' = 'him'. In this poetic line the subject 'جهاندار' (the sovereign) follows the verb phrase for stylistic inversion. 'بنواختش' is an archaic past verb (بنواخت + -ش) meaning 'struck him' or 'smote him' (the preverb ب- appears in older/poetic forms).
segments.s649-g122: 'بر تخت پیروزه' is a prepositional phrase 'upon the (turquoise) throne' (تخت = throne; پیروزه = turquoise/jeweled). 'بنشاختش' is another archaic verbal form (بنشاخت + -ش) meaning 'placed him / seated him there'; the enclitic '-ش' again refers to 'him'. Verb completes the action of placing after the striking.
segments.s649-g123: 'بدو' is the prepositional pronoun 'to him'. 'گفت' here functions as the past of 'to say' ('he said to him'); note that the provided sense-list did not include the 'said' sense so it is mapped separately. 'ز این تاج بی‌بهره‌ام' literally 'of/from this crown I-am-deprived' — بی‌بهره‌ام is an adjectival predicate with the 1st-person suffix '-ام' meaning 'I am bereft/deprived (of it)'.
segments.s649-g124: 'به بی‌بهرگی' means 'for/by (my) lack' or 'on account of being bereft' (به here = 'for'/'because of'). 'در جهان' = 'in the world'. 'شهره‌ام' is the adjectival/passive-like form with 1st-person suffix meaning 'I am famed / I am renowned' — the construction 'به X شهره‌ام' = 'I am known/famed for X.'
segments.s649-g125: 'همه' = 'all/everyone' (quantifier). 'سوفزا' is an archaic/rare noun used in the poem (the provided senses are uncertain) and functions as the group addressed ('the nobles/courtiers' or a named class); 'راست' here is an intensifier/adverb 'truly/indeed'. 'بهر از مهی' is a prepositional phrase 'for the sake of the moon‑like (one)' — مهی (= moon‑like) is a poetic epithet for beauty; بهر/از combine to indicate purpose or motive ('for/from the moon-like one').
segments.s649-g126: 'همی' is an adverb meaning 'still/always'. 'نام بینم' literally 'I see a name' is an idiomatic poetic way of saying 'I obtain/receive a name (i.e., fame)'; 'بینم' is 1st-person present of 'to see' used here in the sense 'I gain/receive'. 'ز شاهنشهی' = 'from/through kingship/imperial rule' (ز marks the source).
segments.s649-g127: 'از این' = 'because of these / from this'. 'داد و بیداد' pairs 'justice (داد)' and 'injustice/oppression (بیداد)' — a fixed poetic antithetical phrase. 'در گردنم' literally 'in/on my neck' is an idiom meaning 'weighing on me' or 'hanging about me' (something imposed on the speaker).
segments.s649-g128: 'به فرجام روزی' is a temporal/teleological phrase: 'به فرجام' = 'to the end/outcome' and روزی in this poetic context conveys the sense 'one day / at a certain day' (the provided sense-list gives 'to dawn/appear' but here it functions adverbially). 'بپیچد' is a present/subjunctive-like form of 'پیچیدن' ('to twist/turn'), used here to indicate that the body will be turned/bring to a close (a future/inevitable event). 'تنم' = 'my body' (تن + enclitic -م); note that the provided sense list for تنم did not include the ordinary sense 'body', so that sense is mapped as unavailable below.
segments.s649-g361: پدرم is the noun پدر with the possessive suffix -م ('my father'). این‌چنین گفت is a past-tense clause ('said thus'); the verb گفت is the past of 'say'. و من این‌چنین is an elliptical continuation ('and I [said] likewise') where the verb is omitted and understood from the previous clause.
segments.s649-g362: که introduces a subordinate content clause ('that ...'). بر is the preposition 'on/upon' governing the name آفریدون. کنیم is the present/subjunctive form of کردن ('we do/let us do') used here as a hortative/subjunctive after that-clause. آفرین is a noun 'acclaim/bravo' functioning as the object of کنیم; the word order is somewhat marked/poetic (verb then object).
segments.s649-g363: ز here is the poetic preposition 'from'/'because of' introducing the cause. گفتار او is a genitive phrase 'his words/speech'. شادتر شد is the past-tense predicate 'became happier' (شادتر = comparative adjective, شد = became). قباد appears after the verb (verb–subject order common in classical Persian poetry).
segments.s649-g364: ز روزی که is a temporal phrase 'from the day that...' (ز = 'from'). روزی in classical Persian here means 'the day' (sense not listed in the provided options). که introduces the relative/time clause. تاج کیی is 'the Kayanian crown' (کیی = Kayanian), and برنهاد is the past transitive verb 'placed on/put on' (he put the crown on).
segments.s649-g365: بسیجید is a past verbal form meaning 'they mustered/gathered' (بسیجیدن = to muster). عماری is the noun directly attached to that verb as the group that mustered (the exact sense of عماری is unclear from the provided choices). و آمد به راه is a simple sequence 'and came/set out on the road' (آمد = came; به راه = on the road).
segments.s649-g366: نشسته is a participial/adjectival form 'seated/sitting' used predicatively; بدو = 'toward him' (direction/attitude), اندرون = 'inside/within'. جفت شاه is the noun phrase 'a pair of kings' (جفت = pair, شاه = king) and functions as the logical subject placed after the participle, so the clause reads 'inside, facing him, a pair of kings was seated' with the copula omitted (typical in poetry).
segments.s649-g367: بیاورد is a past-tense verb 'he brought/led'. لشکر is its direct object 'an army' and سوی طیسفون is the directional phrase 'toward Ctesiphon'. The order (verb–object–direction) is typical of epic/poetic inversion.
segments.s649-g368: دل is the subject 'heart(s)'; از درد ایرانیان is an ablative/genitive phrase 'from the suffering of the Iranians' that gives the cause. پر ز is the idiomatic construction 'full of' (پر = full, followed by ز + noun), and خون is the noun 'blood'/'bloodshed' completing the phrase. segments.s649-g161: چو introduces a temporal clause meaning “when.” بنشست is a past tense verb “sat down”; in classical/poetic Persian the verb can precede the subject, so بنشست شاپور = “Shapur sat down.” با + personal name (سوفزای) means “with Sufzā.”
segments.s649-g162: فراوان is an adverb modifying the verb زدند (“they did/struck/proposed”) and shows frequency/extent. زدند is past plural; here it has the idiomatic sense “hit upon/put forward” (choose sense ‘to strike’ as in ‘strike upon an idea’). از + (بد و نیک رای) marks the content or kind of those acts — “of bad and good counsel,” where رای functions as “counsel/plan.”
segments.s649-g163: بدو is an indirect-object pronoun phrase “to him.” داد is past “gave.” پس is a temporal/sequence adverb “then/after.” نامه شهریار is a possessive/genitive juxtaposition meaning “the sovereign’s (the king’s) letter” (poetry often omits the ezāfe). Word order here (indirect object + verb + adverb + noun phrase) is typical in older/poetic Persian.
segments.s649-g164: سخن رفت is an impersonal idiom: رفت (“went”) with سخن indicates that talk/rumor followed or was carried on — “discussion arose” or “conversation went.” هرگونه modifies the following adjectives and means “of every kind.” دشوار و خوار are adjectives describing the sort of talk: “harsh/difficult and humiliating/despising.”
segments.s649-g165: چو begins a temporal clause “when.” برخواند is past tense “read aloud/recited” (here used transitively). آن نامه را is the definite object with را marking it. پهلوان is the subject “the champion/hero”; again the verb appears before or adjacent to the subject in poetic order: “when the champion read that letter.”
segments.s649-g166: بپژمرد is a past/stative verb meaning “withered/paled” (he grew weak/pale). The conjunction و links clauses; شد is the past of شدن “became,” followed by کند here in the adjective sense “slow/sluggish” (شد کند = “became sluggish”). تیره‌روان is a compound adjective “dark‑souled/gloomy.” The sequence describes a physical and moral collapse caused by the letter.
segments.s649-g167: چو ... برخواند again marks a temporal clause “when he read that letter.” آن نامه is the object; برخواند is past “read aloud.” شاپور is the subject and گفت is the verb “said,” introducing reported speech (followed by که). The order places the verb and reporting verb in the same line typical of narrative poetry.
segments.s649-g168: که introduces reported speech (“that”). اکنون = “now.” سخن را uses را to mark the definite object (“the matter/what is to be spoken of”). نباید is a negative impersonal modal meaning “one must not/it ought not,” and is followed by نهفت (the infinitive/participial form of ‘to hide’), so نباید نهفت = “it should not be concealed.” This is a standard obligation construction in older Persian.
segments.s649-g289: سوی شاه هیتال کردند روی is built around the light verb کردن with روی as its object: روی کردن = 'to turn one's face'. سوی + NP is a classical prepositional phrase meaning 'toward (the)'. The verb کردند is 3rd-person plural past, so the whole chunk reads 'they turned (their) face toward King Hital.'
segments.s649-g290: ز introduces a cause or source here ('from/because of') and governs the following coordinate phrase. اندیشگان خسته is a nominal phrase meaning 'the thoughtful/with thinking, weary' (i.e. 'weary from thinking'); و links to راه جوی, where راه جوی is a compound meaning 'seeking the way' (جوی as 'search'). The whole segment explains why they turned their faces.
segments.s649-g291: بر این‌گونه here is a fixed classical phrase meaning 'in this way' or 'thus'; بر takes the adverbial این‌گونه. سرگشته is a predicative adjective 'bewildered/astray' describing the following noun phrase آن هفت مرد ('those seven men'), where آن is the demonstrative and هفت مرد is 'seven men' (here with heroic/epic coloring).
segments.s649-g292: به اهواز رفتند is straightforward: به + place = 'to Ahvaz' and رفتند is 3rd-person plural past 'they went'. تازان چو گرد is a simile/adverbial phrase modifying رفتند: تازان (fierce/swift ones) + چو 'like' + گرد 'dust' (i.e. 'like a cloud/dust-whirl'), so it means they rushed off like a whirlwind.
segments.s649-g293: رسیدند is 3rd-person plural past 'they arrived'. پویان stands as the subject 'the seekers/those on a quest' (an agentive/epic noun). به پرمایه ده is a prepositional phrase 'to the fruitful/substantial village' (پرمایه modifying ده). Word order is verb → subject → complement, typical of classical prose/poetic flexibility.
segments.s649-g294: به ده در literally 'to the village door' (به + ده + در, where در functions as 'door/household entrance'). یکی نامبردار is 'a certain renowned/celebrated (one)'; نامبردار modifies the implied person at that door. مه here is poetic and functions as an intensifier/epithet ('great' / 'mickle') — the phrase names a single notable at whose door they arrive.
segments.s649-g295: بدان here is the classical compound meaning 'to that' (ب = to/into + دان as the rest of the compound); خان دهقان is a genitival/title phrase 'the khan (lord) peasant' or 'the peasant-chieftain' (خان used as a local lord/inn or honorific attached to دهقان). فرود آمدند is a verbal compound meaning 'they alighted/descended' (فرود = descent/alighting + آمدند 'they came'), so the clause means 'they alighted at that khan/peasant's (place).'
segments.s649-g296: ببودند is an archaic/poetic past plural form meaning 'they were / they stayed'. و links the next phrase: یک هفته دم برزدند is an adverbial time clause 'for one week' (یک هفته) followed by دم برزدند, an idiomatic sequence where دم ('breath' / 'a moment') + برزدند ('they struck/beat') conveys that they lingered or passed the time there for a week. Functionally the segment reports that they remained for a week.
segments.s649-g305: بشد تیز (بشد + تیز) is a compact past‑tense idiom meaning 'he became quick' → 'he hurried'. و is the simple connective 'and/then'. رازش is راز + ـش (possessive) 'his secret'. به دهقان بگفت is 'he told (بگفت, past) to the dehqan' with به marking the indirect object.
