segments.s649-g306: که introduces the reported content clause 'that'. این دخترت را is 'this daughter of yours' with را as the definite/object marker. کسی نیست جفت literally 'there is no one [who is] a match' — کسی here takes the sense 'no one' under the negation (نیست), and جفت functions as predicate 'match/mate'.
segments.s649-g307: یکی here is the indefinite 'a certain' (a person). پاک is an epithet 'pure/upright'. انبازش is an archaic/uncertain word (with the possessive ‑ش) — contextually an envoy/messenger; آمد is the past 'came'. به جای means 'in place' or 'in his stead'.
segments.s649-g308: که here introduces the purpose/destination clause. گردی (a verbal noun/nominal of movement) is used as 'a going/turn/journey' (sense 'wandering' → here 'journey/mission'). بر + اهواز marks the destination 'to/at Ahvaz', and the second بر + کدخدای specifies 'to the kadkhoda (village headman)'.
segments.s649-g309: گرانمایه is an honorific adjective 'esteemed/valuable' modifying دهقان (the dehqan). به زرمهر گفت = 'he said/told to Zarmehr', with به marking the indirect object and گفت as the past-tense verb 'said'.
segments.s649-g310: که introduces the reported clause. این دختر خوب را = 'this daughter, (who is) beautiful/ good', with را as object marker. نیست جفت = 'there is not a match' — نیست negates existence and جفت is predicate 'match/mate'.
segments.s649-g311: اگر (if) + شاید (perhaps) sets a tentative conditional. این مرد = 'this man'. فرمان تو راست combines 'فرمان (your command) + تو (your)' with راست 'true/right' — idiomatically here 'is faithful/true to your command' or 'obeys you'. Note the older order (noun + possessive + predicate adjective).
segments.s649-g312: مر is the dative 'to me' (archaic position before the object). این را 'this (thing)' is the direct object. ب + دان is the prefix be‑ plus دان 'to know' (i.e. بدان 'make known' or 'know'); ده is imperative of 'to give' functioning here with بدان to mean 'let (it) be made known / inform me'. که introduces the content: او را (object‑marked) + هواست = 'he has desire' (هوا = desire/passion + ست copula).
segments.s649-g281: This is a compressed, poetic word order: دارم (present 1st sg. 'I have/possess') is the main verb, and سپاس ('thanks') is placed after it for emphasis. Normally in modern Persian one would say «از تو سپاس دارم» ('I thank you'), but Ferdowsi inverts and front‑positions the item received «همین تاج و تخت» ('this very crown and throne') before the clause describing gratitude «از تو دارم سپاس». دارم is present indicative (speaker's stance) rather than a past tense.
segments.s649-g282: This clause omits the copula (ام 'am') typical of classical Persian poetry: the speaker is identified as «بوم جاودانه» ('an inhabitant/of the eternal abode') and «تو را حق‑شناس» means 'grateful to you' (تو را marks the recipient of gratitude). Word order places the noun phrase before the object/recipient and the adjective; the implied verb 'am' is left out for poetic terseness.
segments.s649-g283: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' and بشنید is past tense 'he heard'. زر مهر پاکیزه‌رای is a compact noun phrase: زر 'gold' governed by مهر 'love/kindness' modified by پاکیزه‌رای 'pure‑minded/pure‑favour' — in poetry the usual ezāfe links are condensed, so read it as 'the gold of pure affection'.
segments.s649-g284: This is an object phrase plus verb: سبک بند ('a light fetter/strap') is made definite by را, and برگشادش ('he opened/loosened it') is past with the enclitic pronoun -ش referring back to بند. ز پای ('from the foot') gives the source. Note برگشاد combines the prefix بر‑ + گشاد ('open') — a classical perfective form.
segments.s649-g285: فرستاد is simple past 'he sent'. The conjunction و links the next action. آن پنج تن را بخواند uses بخواند in the sense 'he called/summoned' (classical past form); را marks 'those five' as the definite object. In classical usage بخواند can function as 'summoned' rather than literally 'read'.
segments.s649-g286: همه رازها ('all the secrets') is the theme, پیش ایشان ('before them / in their presence') is a locative phrase, and براند is a classical verb meaning here 'set forth / brought before' (an extension of راندن 'to drive/bring'). The verb completes the clause: he presented or disclosed all the secrets to them.
segments.s649-g287: شب تیره ('dark night') is a temporal setting. از شهر بیرون شدند is a compound verbal idea 'they went out of the city' — بیرون شدن + شدند (past plural of شدن) marks the departure. In classical narrative شدن is often used with motion verbs to indicate an action performed by the subject.
segments.s649-g288: ز دیدار دشمن is a causal/temporal phrase 'because/on account of seeing the enemy' (ز here = 'from/on the occasion of'), and به هامون شدند means 'they went/arrived to the plain' with به marking direction and شدند the past plural. The clauses are compactly juxtaposed to show cause and consequent movement.
segments.s649-g353: پسر (noun) 'son' is the patient noun; زاد here is the verb (archaic/poetic past) 'was born' or 'begot' placed directly after the noun as in older Persian. جفت تو ('your jift') is a genitive phrase 'your mate/consort' (جفت = spouse/mate) attached to the verb phrase; در شب یکی is a temporal prepositional phrase 'on one night' (در = in/on, شب = night, یکی = one) with the numeral after the noun as in poetic word order.
segments.s649-g354: که is the subordinating conjunction 'when/that' introducing a temporal clause. از ماه is a partitive/locative 'of the moon' (i.e. as regards the moon). پیدا نبود is a compound past negation 'was not visible' (پیدا = visible; نبود = was not). اندکی is a small-quantity adverb 'a little' placed after the negation to give the sense 'not even a little' (poetic emphasis).
segments.s649-g355: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. بشنید is past tense 'they/he heard' (here impersonal/past: 'when it was heard' or 'when they heard'). در خانه is the locative 'in the house/household'. شد is the past of 'become'. شادکام is a predicative adjective 'joyful/fortunate' describing the household.
segments.s649-g356: همانگاه is an adverb 'immediately'. کسریش is the name کسری (Kisra/Kasra) with the third-person clitic -ش 'him' attached, so the phrase functions as the object 'him Kasra' (i.e. 'they called him Kasra'). کردند is plural past 'they did/made' and نام 'name' is the noun; together کرد ند نام = 'they made (gave) [the] name' i.e. 'they named him'. Note: کسری here is a proper name/title; the provided lexical senses in the lemma list do not match that use.
segments.s649-g357: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. دهقان is the addressee/source 'the peasant' (object of 'ask'). بپرسید is past tense 'asked'. زان پس (زان = 'from that'; پس = 'after/then') is a temporal connector 'after that/thereupon'. قباد (Qobad) is the subject; word order is inverted for poetic effect (subject appears at the end).
segments.s649-g358: که‌ای is a vocative/introductory particle here rendering 'O ...' (addressing the boy) — poetically written as که‌ای. نیکبخت is the vocative 'fortunate one'. از که is the interrogative 'from whom' (از = from; که = who/whom). داری is present-tense 'you have/possess' (here 'do you have' meaning 'are you of [what] lineage'). نژاد is 'lineage/stock'. Together: 'O fortunate one, from whom do you have lineage?'
segments.s649-g359: بدو گفت is the fixed reporting phrase 'he said to him' (بدو = to him; گفت = said). کز is the conjunction 'that' introducing the reported clause. آفریدون is the proper name 'Afaridun/Afrīdūn'. گرد here is poetic/archaic and in this context indicates kinship/association with the named figure (i.e. 'of Afrīdūn's line' or 'connected to Afrīdūn'); the exact sense is archaic/poetic and does not match the modern dictionary senses given in the word list.
segments.s649-g360: که is the subordinating conjunction 'that'. از تخم ضحاک is a genitive/partitive phrase 'from the seed/stock of Zahhāk' (از = from; تخم = seed/origin; ضحاک = proper name). شاهی is 'kingship' (here the abstract noun 'royal rule'). ببرد is a present/future-oriented verb form 'will take/win' (here 'will obtain' or 'will wrest (the kingship)'). The clause states that from Zahhāk's seed kingship will be gained/taken.
segments.s649-g129: به ایران is a dative/prepositional phrase 'to/for Iran' placed before the rest for emphasis. برادر is the noun 'brother' (here meaning a person regarded as a brother/kinsman), and بدی is used adjectivally/value‑judgmentally ('bad' / 'harmful' / 'evil') modifying برادر; کدخدای is an appositive or role-label 'the village‑headman'. The verb is omitted (elliptical past action is implied by the context), so this whole clause reads as an asserted appointment: 'you gave/appointed a bad brother to Iran as kâdkhudâ.'
segments.s649-g130: به هستی ... is a poetic circumlocution: به هستی 'in existence / while (someone) lives' + ز introduces the agent/reason. ز here means 'from / by reason of / owing to' (poetic use), followed by the modifier بیدادگر 'the oppressor/tyrant' and the proper name سوفزای. Together the segment gives the circumstantial clause 'while/so long as the tyrant Soofzây exists/was alive.'
segments.s649-g131: بدو is the dative phrase 'to him' (to that person). گفت is the verb 'said' (past) — here used in the simple past and placed after the indirect object (a common poetic word order). شاپور is the speaker's name; کای is the title 'Kay' (a royal epithet) used before شهریار 'king' (شهریار appositive/epithet). The sequence is inverted for poetic emphasis: 'To him said Shâhpur, Kay, the king.'
segments.s649-g132: دلت را is 'your heart' with the possessive suffix -ت on دل plus the object/topic marker را (literally 'your heart' as the thing being addressed). بدین کار is the prepositional phrase 'about/over this matter' (بدین = بِ + این 'by/with this', کار = 'matter/affair'). رنجه مدار is a negative admonition: رنجه (participle/verb root related to رنجیدن 'to be distressed') + مدار used as a prohibitive 'do not...' (a fixed poetic negative construction), yielding 'do not distress/afflict your heart' or 'do not grieve about this.'
segments.s649-g133: یکی نامه literally 'one letter' (a single / a certain letter). باید + نوشتن is a modal construction: باید 'it is necessary / one must' followed by the infinitive نوشتن 'to write' — common Persian order places باید before the infinitive. درشت is an adjective 'large/coarse/plain/strong' qualifying نامه: 'a blunt / forceful letter.'
segments.s649-g134: تو را is a topicalized/ emphatic 'you' (تو with the object/topic marker را). Then a noun series نام و فر و نژاد و پشت lists the things possessed: نام 'name/reputation', فر 'farr/royal glory', نژاد (here in the verbal/compound form نژادست) 'lineage/noble birth' and پشت 'backing/support'. Grammatically the list is presented as what the addressee possesses or can claim in the letter.
segments.s649-g135: بگویی is a jussive/subjunctive form often used to mean 'you should say' or 'say that' (optative/imperative flavor in Persian poetic diction). که introduces the content clause 'that ...'. از تخت شاهنشاهی is a prepositional phrase 'from/of the throne of kingship/empire' — از marks the source or cause (here: 'with regard to / because of the imperial throne').
segments.s649-g136: مرا بهره رنجست is a verbless/nominal clause with an older copula: بهره 'portion/share' is predicated as رنجست 'is suffering/tribulation' and مرا gives the person affected ('for me' / 'my'). The sequence yields 'my portion is suffering' (i.e. 'my share is grief'). و گنج تهی joins a second predicate: 'and the treasury (or treasure) is empty.' تهی 'empty' is a simple adjective predicative to گنج.
segments.s649-g249: جوانی is a noun meaning 'a youth' (here indefinite). بی‌آزار is an adjectival phrase 'harmless/inoffensive' modifying جوانی. و links that description to the following appositive زرمهر نام: نام (literally 'name') follows the proper name زرمهر to form the stock phrase 'called Zarmehr'. Word order is noun → modifiers/apposition, typical in classical Persian.
segments.s649-g250: که introduces a relative clause 'who/whose...'. از here has the causal sense 'because of' (i.e. 'on account of'). مهر is 'affection/love' and او is a possessive third‑person pronoun 'his'. بد in this line is not the adjective 'bad' but the past copula (بُد = 'was'); it functions as 'was' linking to the predicate شادکام 'fortunate'. پدر is the subject of that copula and شادکام is a predicate adjective 'fortunate'.
segments.s649-g251: سپردند is a 3rd‑person plural past tense 'they entrusted/handed over'. بسته is a past participle/adjectival complement 'bound/with hands tied', placed after the verb. بدو is the dative 'to him'. شاه را is the definite object 'the king' marked by را. The sequence is verb → adjectival complement → indirect object → direct object (object ordering is flexible in Persian poetry).
segments.s649-g252: بدان گونه is a frozen locution meaning 'in that manner' (ب + آن → 'in that', گونه = 'manner, way'). بد here is the adjective 'bad/evil' modifying رای, so بد رای = 'evil counsel/plan'. بدخواه is 'ill‑wisher' (one who wishes ill). را at the end is the accusative/definiteness marker applied to بدخواه, giving emphasis or marking it as the affected party of the preceding phrase.
segments.s649-g253: که is the relative marker 'who/that'. آن مهربان is 'that kind/kindhearted one' (آن = 'that/the'). کینه (here a noun) means 'grudge/hatred' and is linked to the proper name سوفزای in a genitive relation: 'the grudge of Sofuzāy'. Word order places the possessor after the possessed, which is common in poetry.
segments.s649-g254: بخواهد is the present/future form of خواستن 'to want/wish' (3sg): here 'would/will wish'. به درد is a prepositional phrase literally 'to/into pain' (marking the kind of harm). از جهان is a prepositional phrase 'out of the world' (i.e. remove from the world). کدخدای is the object 'the village‑headman' — the whole phrase says someone desires to cause the kadehdāy to be taken out of the world by pain.
segments.s649-g255: This is a string of epithets in apposition: بی‌آزار 'harmless' modifies the proper name زرمهر; یزدان‌پرست is an epithet 'God‑worshipper/pious' that likewise apposes the name. Such stacked epithets are common in epic Persian and serve to characterize the person before the clause continues.
segments.s649-g256: نسودی is a family name or epithet attached to the hero. به بد 'به' + noun 'بد' = 'toward/into evil' (i.e. 'in wrongdoing'). با جهاندار دست is an idiom: 'دست با X داشتن' = 'to join hands/ally with X'; جهاندار is 'the sovereign' and دست 'hand' is used idiomatically for cooperation. The clause therefore says he did not ally himself with the ruler in evil (the negative sense is supplied by the context/poetic ellipsis).
segments.s649-g377: بیاریم is a 1st‑person plural hortative/subjunctive ('let us bring') built on آوردن but here given in an irregular poetic form; جاماسب ده‌ساله را is a noun phrase with the name جاماسب followed by the age adjective ده‌ساله ('ten‑year‑old') and the enclitic را marking the whole phrase as a definite/direct object. The object is what the verb 'let us bring' takes.
segments.s649-g378: که introduces a purposive/subordinate clause here ('so that' / 'in order that'); با is the preposition 'with' or 'by (means of)'; در همتا is a small noun phrase where همتا 'match/peer' is the key noun and در is a preceding element (poetic/elliptical here, part of the instrumental expression); کند is the 3rd‑person singular present/subjunctive of کردن ('to make/do') used to indicate the action in the subordinate clause; ژاله را is the definite object (ژاله as the person/item to be matched, marked by را). The clause therefore expresses the intended result/purpose of bringing Jamasp.
segments.s649-g379: مگرمان (مگر + مان) functions here as a concessive/conditional 'unless' or rhetorical 'should we not' introducing what is to be avoided; ز is the poetic preposition 'from' (here indicating cause or source); تاراج و خون ریختن is a nominalized phrase where تاراج 'pillage' and خون ریختن 'shedding of blood' are parallel objects (ریختن is the verbal noun meaning 'to spill/shed'). Together they name the violent acts to be avoided.
segments.s649-g380: به یک‌سو گراییم is a present/subjunctive‑colored clause 'we incline/turn to one side' (به + یک‌سو = 'to one side', گراییم is 1st‑person plural present/subjunctive/optative of گرا داشتن/گرا بودن 'to incline'); ز آویختن ('from hanging/attaching') uses ز again to mark separation or avoidance and the verbal noun آویختن ('to cling/hang on'), so the whole phrase expresses turning aside away from clinging (figuratively: abandoning mutual dependence or a previous stance).
segments.s649-g381: برفتند is the simple past 3rd‑person plural 'they went/left'; یکسر here is an adverb modifying the movement ('all at once/straightaway/entirely'); سوی + name (سوی کیقباد) marks the direction 'toward Kay Qobad'—together the segment describes the group moving as a unit toward the king.
segments.s649-g382: بگفتند is the past 3rd‑person plural 'they said' (introducing direct speech); کای is a vocative particle 'O' used before royal titles; شاه خسرونژاد is a compound vocative/title meaning 'king, scion/descendant of Khosrow'—the whole segment is the conventional address used when speaking to the king.
segments.s649-g383: گر is the conditional 'if'; از تو marks the source/target of the feeling 'from you'; دل مردمان 'the hearts of the people' is the subject phrase; خسته شد is the simple past/passive sense 'became weary' (شد as auxiliary/lexical 'became' + adjective خسته). The clause states a hypothetical: 'If the people's hearts became tired of you...'.
segments.s649-g384: به شوخی here uses به with an abstract noun to indicate the means/agent ('by/through jest/play'); دل و دیدها 'hearts and sights' are coordinated objects/topics; شسته شد is a passive construction 'were washed/cleansed' (شسته as past participle + شد 'became/was'), meaning metaphorically that by jest the emotions and perceptions were cleansed or refreshed. The phrase links as the consequent to the protasis introduced by گر.
segments.s649-g297: This is an archaic/poetic SOV clause with an initial indefinite 'یکی' (= 'a certain'). 'یکی دختری داشت دهقان' literally 'a certain daughter the farmer had' — 'دهقان' is the subject (the farmer) and 'داشت' is the past-tense verb 'had'. The final phrase 'چو ماه' is a simile modifying 'دختری' (the daughter), 'چو' = 'like/as' and 'ماه' = 'moon' (i.e. very beautiful).
segments.s649-g298: 'ز' is the poetic preposition 'from/with' marking source or quality; here 'ز مشک سیه' describes a dark (سیه) musk-related mark or staining. 'بر سرش' = 'upon her head', where '-ش' is the enclitic possessive 'her'. The repeated 'بر' before 'کلاه' stacks local markers: 'on her head, on (top of) the cap' — a common poetic layering for location.
segments.s649-g299: 'جهانجوی' is an epithet/noun ('the world-seeker') placed as subject. 'چون' here functions as a temporal conjunction 'when/as', and 'روی دختر' is the object phrase 'the girl's face' (noun + modifier). 'بدید' is the past tense 'saw'. Word order is subject + temporal conjunction + object + verb, a poetic ordering.
segments.s649-g300: 'ز' marks source ('from'), so 'ز مغز جوان' = 'from the mind/brain of the young (man)'. 'شد' is the inchoative/past auxiliary 'became' and introduces the result clause; poetic inversion places the verb before the predicate 'خرد ناپدید' which literally reads 'wisdom vanished' ('خرد' = wisdom, 'ناپدید' = vanished). Overall: 'wisdom became vanished from the young man's mind.'
segments.s649-g301: 'همانگه' is an adverb meaning 'at that very moment' or 'immediately'. 'بیامد' is the past tense 'he came'. 'به زرمهر' is the prepositional phrase 'to Zarmahr' (proper name). 'گفت' is the past-tense verb 'said' introducing the following content clause.
segments.s649-g302: 'که' introduces the reported content ('that'). 'با تو سخن دارم' is an idiomatic expression literally 'I have speech with you' = 'I have something to tell/say to you' — 'دارم' here is the present-tense first-person used inside reported speech. 'اندر نهفت' is an adverbial phrase 'in secret / privately' (اندر = in/within, نهفت = hidden/concealed).
segments.s649-g303: Imperative 'برو' = 'go' + object 'راز من' = 'my secret'. 'پیش دهقان' = 'in the presence of the farmer' (پیش = before/in front of, here 'to the farmer'). 'بگوی' is a jussive/imperative-form verb 'tell' (classical/poetic form instead of the colloquial 'بگو'), giving the command to relay the secret.
segments.s649-g304: 'مگر' here introduces a question of possibility (roughly 'to see whether' / 'ask if'); it signals a hoped-for condition. 'جفت من گردد' = 'become my match' where 'گردد' is the classical/subjunctive-like form of 'become' (may become). 'این خوبروی' ('this fair-faced one') is the person in question and appears after the verb due to poetic inversion: literally 'ask (whether) my mate becomes this fair-faced.'
segments.s649-g65: گر begins a conditional clause 'if'. از من is a genitive/prepositional phrase 'about/of me' (از governing the pronoun من). کسی is the indefinite subject 'someone'; زشت here modifies گوید meaning 'says something ugly/insulting' (an adjective used with a speech-verb). گوید is the third-person singular narrative/present (epic present) 'says'. The final item بدوی is an archaic/poetic adverbial/compound form attached to the clause and modifies the manner of saying; its exact sense is irregular in the provided lexicon.
segments.s649-g66: ورا is an object pronoun in oblique position 'to him/unto him'. سرد is used adverbially/attributively here ('coldly' or 'aloofly') and precedes the verb گوید ('speaks'), giving the instruction to answer him in a cold way. براند is a present/epic third-person verb 'drives away' (or 'banishes'); ز + روی is a poetic prepositional phrase 'from (the) face/presence' meaning 'from your presence'. The two verbs form a sequence of actions directed at the person who spoke ill.
segments.s649-g67: همی is an iterative/continuous particle typical of epic pasts (marks habitual or ongoing action). باژ 'cry' is the noun and جستی here functions as the verb phrase 'uttered/let out' (but the supplied senses for جستی do not match this use exactly). ز هر کشوری is the source phrase 'from every country/land', with poetic preposition ز + noun.
segments.s649-g68: This is a linked prepositional series repeating ز 'from' for emphasis: ز هر نامداری و هر مهتری = 'from every renowned one and every chief'. Each هر is the distributive determiner 'every/each', نامداری names fame/notability in this catalogue sense, and مهتری denotes chiefs or preeminent persons.
segments.s649-g69: چو introduces a temporal clause here meaning 'when'. آگاهی (information/news) is the subject and آمد is the verb 'came/reached' (epic past/present sense). به سوی قباد is a directional phrase 'toward/to Qobad'; سوی adds an explicit directional nuance.
segments.s649-g70: This continues the source and content of the news: ز شیراز = 'from Shiraz'. The following وز is an archaic fused form (likely و + ز, 'and from') introducing the next source/theme; کار is 'affair/deed' and governs the pair بیداد و داد 'injustice and justice' (two contrasted affairs reported). The phrasing is poetic and repeats the prepositional z to link sources and topics.
segments.s649-g71: همی‌گفت is an iterative narrative verb 'kept saying' or 'would say' used to report a common refrain. هر کس که 'whoever' introduces a relative/conditional notion; جز here means 'except/only' and takes the noun phrase نام شاه 'the name/title of king' (i.e. 'only the title of king'). The clause asserts a limitation on possession or substance behind the title.
segments.s649-g72: ندارد is the negative present/epic-form 'has not' or 'does not possess'. The repeated ز phrases (ز ایران ز گنج و سپاه) list what the person lacks: 'of Iran' (no share in the land), 'of treasure' and 'of army'—z is the poetic genitive/partitive marker here, linking the nouns as possessions the subject lacks.
segments.s649-g145: نویسنده is the person being referred to (here the scribe/copyist) and appears before the verb; نامه را is the definite direct object marked by را. خواندند is past plural 'they read' used here in a causative/performative sense (’they had/ordered him to read’ or simply ‘they caused the reading’); the omitted pronoun object of بنشاندند in the next clause is understood to be this نویسنده.
segments.s649-g146: به نزدیک is a prepositional phrase meaning 'near, at the side of'; شاپور is the proper-name anchor of that phrase. بنشاندند is past plural 'they seated/installed' with an implied object ('him' = the scribe) — standard classical Persian allows verb before explicit subject in such narrative lines.
segments.s649-g147: بگفت is past 'he said/recited'; آن سخنها is the definite object 'those words'. که introduces a relative clause 'which/that', and با شاه گفت functions as 'said to/with the king' (i.e. words that had been used in the king’s presence or addressed to him). Word order (verb then relative clause) is typical of epic narrative.
segments.s649-g148: شد is the copular/predicative 'became/was'. آن کلک بیجاده is a noun plus postpositive adjective ('that pen/klak that is without a road' — poetic phrasing); با introduces the accompanying element and قار جفت means 'paired with a harsh caw' (جفت as predicate 'paired/paired off'). The whole clause describes a poetic image: the pen became matched with a harsh cry.
segments.s649-g149: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. The prepositional phrase بر نامه‌بر مهر بنهاد literally 'placed a مهر (seal) upon the نامه‌بر (letter‑carrier/messenger)'; بنهاد is the past verb 'placed/put' and شاه at the end is the agent 'the king' — classical Persian often places the subject after the verb and its object phrases.
segments.s649-g150: بیاورد here is past 'he brought/put into motion' (rendered 'set on the march'); شاپور is the subject. لشکر به راه is a standard idiom 'the army on the road' = 'the army on the march.' The clause order (verb before subject) is a normal epic/poetic inversion.
segments.s649-g151: گزین کرد is a compound perfective 'he chose/selected' (گزین = choose, کرد = did/made). پس is a discourse adverb 'then/afterward'; هرکه is an indefinite-relative 'whoever' and بد نامدار is an adjectival phrase 'bad‑named' = 'ill‑famed/notorious' describing those selected.
segments.s649-g152: پراگنده is a predicate/adjectival participle 'scattered' describing those people; از introduces the source 'from', لشکر 'the army', and شهریار 'of the king' — together 'scattered from the king's army'. Grammatically this segment completes the description of هرکه: 'whoever was ill‑famed, scattered from the king's army.'
segments.s649-g49: همه کار او = “in all his affairs”: همه (here) modifies کار as ‘entire/whole’; او is the subject ‘he’. پهلوان راندی is a past action phrase where راندی is the past form of ‘to drive’ and takes پهلوان as its object, so ‘he drove (away) champions’. Word order is topic (همه کار) → subject (او) → object+verb (پهلوان راندی).
segments.s649-g50: کسی را بر شاه ننشاندی = ‘he did not seat anyone upon the king/throne’: کسی را is the indefinite object with the object-marker را, بر شاه marks the position/office (‘upon the king’ / ‘on the throne’), and ننشاندی is the past negative of ‘to seat/place’ (ن- + نشاندی), meaning ‘did not seat/place’. The negative prefix applies to this verb phrase.
segments.s649-g51: نه موبد بد او را نه فرمان روای = the first نه opens a neither/ nor construction. موبد is the mōbed/priest; بد (here) functions as ‘to do evil/harm’ with او را as its object (‘do harm to him’), and the second نه introduces the second conjunct (to be completed by the next segment). روای attaches to فرمان to describe the nature of the rule/governance that follows.
segments.s649-g52: جهان بد به دستوری سوفزای = the second half of the nor-clause: جهان بد = ‘the world (was) bad/ill’ (the prior نه negates the badness), and به دستوری سوفزای = ‘by the command/order of Sofuzay’ with به introducing the agent/instrument (‘by/with’) and دستوری as ‘by-command’ (i.e. ‘by order’). Overall: ‘nor, by Sofuzay’s command, was the world’s governance made bad.’
segments.s649-g53: چنین بود = a set phrase ‘thus it was’. تا introduces the limit ‘until’; بیست و سه ساله گشت = ‘he became twenty‑three years old’, where گشت is the past of ‘become’ and ساله is the adjectival ‘‑year‑old’. The numeral elements (بیست و سه) are understood as the quantity modified by ساله.
segments.s649-g54: به جام اندرون باده چون لاله گشت = locative and simile: به جام اندرون باده literally ‘into/at the cup, inside, the wine’ (به as locative/toward, اندرون ‘inside’, باده ‘wine’), چون + لاله = ‘like a tulip’, and گشت again ‘became’. The clause describes a transformation: ‘he became like a tulip in the wine in the cup.’
segments.s649-g55: بیامد = simple past ‘came’. بر + noun here marks direction ‘to/toward’ (بر تاجور سوفزای = ‘to the merchant Sofuzay’), with تاجور as the noun ‘merchant’ and سوفزای the name in apposition/genitive.
segments.s649-g56: به دستوری بازگشتن به جای = ‘with/by the order (دستوری) to return (بازگشتن) to the place’ (به جای = ‘to the place/position’). به دستوری uses به with an instrumental/agentive sense (‘by/with an order’), and بازگشتن is the infinitive ‘to return’, functioning as the content of that order.
segments.s649-g273: چو here is a conditional/temporal conjunction ('if'/'when') introducing the report; گویی is the present form of گفتن meaning 'you say' but the provided senses list does not include this verbal sense (so marked below). به سوگند is an instrument/phrase 'by an oath' (به + noun), and پیمان کنم uses the verb کنم ('I will make') with پیمان ('covenant/pledge') to mean 'I will make/take a pledge'.
segments.s649-g274: که introduces the subordinate reported clause 'that ...'. هرگز is the adverb 'never'. وفای تو را is a genitive construction where وفا ('fidelity') is possessed by تو ('you') and را marks it as the definite object. نشکنم is the negative present/subjunctive (نـ + شکنم) meaning 'I will not break' (شکنم from شکست/شکن 'break').
segments.s649-g275: Syntax is somewhat topical: از او ('from him') opens the clause, ایمنی یافت is a compound verb phrase 'found/obtained safety' (یافت 'obtained/found'), and جان قباد is the noun phrase 'Qobad's life' where جان is the patient/theme and قباد is the subject/name following it (poetic word order).
segments.s649-g276: ز گفتار آن is a prepositional phrase 'from that speech'; پر خرد is a compound adjective/noun phrase meaning 'full of wisdom' (پر = 'full (of)', خرد = 'wisdom') describing the person, گشت is past of گشتن used as 'became', and شاد is the resulting predicate adjective 'glad'.
segments.s649-g277: وز آن پس is an archaic/poetic sequence meaning 'and after that' (وز here is a fossilized conjunctional form). بدو means 'to him' (indirect object), راز بگشاد is a past action 'he opened/revealed the secret' (ب- prefix + گشاد), و گفت is 'and said' introducing reported speech.
segments.s649-g278: که introduces the reported clause. اندیشه here means 'intention' or 'purpose' and از تو gives the target/source 'from you' (i.e. 'from your knowledge' or 'from you'). تخواهم is an archaic/poetic first‑person future auxiliary construction ('I will ...') and نهفت is from نهفتن 'to hide/conceal' (here used as the lexical verb); the clause thus expresses 'I will (not) hide my intention from you' (the older word order places از تو before the verbs).
segments.s649-g279: گشادست is a predicative/poetic form meaning 'is opened' or 'is revealed' (here stative), بر پنج تن marks the beneficiaries/recipients 'to five persons' (بر + number + تن where تن = 'person'), and راز من is the possessive 'my secret'. The clause uses poetic order with the predicate before or adjacent to the recipient phrase.
segments.s649-g280: جز این 'except this' sets up the restriction; نشنود is the negative 3rd‑person form of شنیدن 'does not hear' (here general/gnomic negative), followed by the subject یک تن 'one person' in V–S order (typical poetic inversion), and آواز من 'my voice' is the object/thing not heard. The whole means 'none of them, except this one, hears my voice.'
segments.s649-g345: بپذرفت is a past-tense third-person form (archaic/poetic variant of پذیرفت) meaning 'he/she accepted/received'. In this clause the verb precedes the noun phrase that follows for poetic word order. شمشیرزن (literally 'swordsman') is the head noun being counted, and سی‌هزار (the numeral) follows it here (poetic inversion of the usual 'سی‌هزار شمشیرزن'), together meaning 'thirty thousand swordsmen'.
segments.s649-g346: همه is a quantifier 'all/every' modifying the following noun. نامداران is a plural substantive formed from the adjective 'renowned' (used here as 'the renowned ones'). گرد و سوار is a coordinated description 'گرد and سوار'—سوار is clearly 'rider/horseman'; گرد in this text is part of the paired phrase and its usual listed senses ('around', 'dust', 'to go about') do not capture a specialized martial sense, so it should be read as forming a paired opposition with سوار (i.e. foot and horse) in the poetic context.
segments.s649-g347: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. هیتالیان is a proper plural name (here a people/place/clan) serving as the origin. سوی is the directional preposition 'toward', and اهواز is the place-name 'Ahvaz'. شد is the past intransitive verb here meaning that the movement/event 'happened' or 'set out' (poetic usage of شد).
segments.s649-g348: سراسر جهان is an adverbial phrase 'throughout the world'. The sequence ز + و in the text functions as the contracted 'از او' = 'from him' (here tokenized as ز and و separately; و in this role is a clitic pronoun referring back to the person), and پر + آواز literally 'full of voice' = 'filled with news/fame'. شد is the copular/predicative past 'became' ('was filled').
segments.s649-g349: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. نزدیکی is a noun 'nearness/approach' used adverbially ('at the approach of'). خان دهقان is a titular phrase 'the khan (lord) Dehqān' or 'the khan of the dehqāns' (title + social class). رسید is the past verb 'arrived/reached'. The whole clause means 'when the khan‑dehqān came near/arrived'.
segments.s649-g350: بسی here functions adverbially as 'many'/'much' (an archaic usage) modifying مردم 'people'. از خانه بیرون is the phrase 'out of the house(s)' (از = 'from/out of', خانه = 'house'), and دوید is the past of دویدن 'ran/ran out' (intransitive). The clause states that many people ran outside from their houses.
segments.s649-g351: یکی is an indefinite 'someone/a certain one'. مژده is 'good news/tidings'. بردند is the past plural 'they carried/brought' (indefinite plural frequently used for 'was brought' in narrative). نزد means 'to/in the presence of' (i.e. 'to Qobad'), and قباد is the proper name Qobad — together 'someone brought the news to Qobad.'
segments.s649-g352: که introduces the content clause ('that/that...'). این پور = 'this son' (این = 'this', پور = 'son/descendant'). بر here functions as a prepositional phrase 'upon/for' (poetic phrasing: 'be auspicious upon the king'). شاه = 'the king'. فرخنده is an adjective 'auspicious/fortunate'. باد at the end is the optative particle 'may it be' (wish), a conventional closing for benedictions in classical Persian.
segments.s649-g265: ز دل‌پاک = a prepositional phrase 'from (the) pure heart' (ز = poetic 'from'); بردارم is first-person singular present/subjunctive (bardāram) meaning 'I remove / I will remove' and here expresses the speaker's intention; آزار تو follows as the object 'your pain/harm' (آزار with the possessor تو). Word order is topical prepositional phrase → verb → object, typical in older Persian poetry.
segments.s649-g266: کنم is first-person singular present/subjunctive 'I make / I will make'; چشم روشن is a light collocation 'bright eye(s)' meaning 'delighted/cheered eyes' (چشم = 'eye'); به دیدار تو is a dative/locative phrase 'at/for the meeting (sight) of you'. The verb کنم takes 'چشم روشن' as what is made and 'به دیدار تو' as the occasion ('I will make my eyes bright on seeing you').
segments.s649-g267: بدو گفت = 'to him said' (word order inverted): بدو is 'to him/unto him' and گفت is the verb 'said' (here used as past 'he said'); زرمهر is the speaker's name; کای شهریار is a vocative phrase 'O king' (کای = 'O'). The sequence marks direct speech introduced by Zarmehr addressing the king.
segments.s649-g268: زبان را = 'the tongue / your speech' marked with را (definite/object marker) as the thing being affected; بدین (here written as ب + دین) = 'in this way / thus' (ب functioning as the prepositional/prefixal element 'be-/with'), باز = 'again' or 'thus' in the sense 'so/thus' forming the adverbial phrase 'in this way/so'; رنجه مدار is a prohibitive phrase meaning 'do not afflict/distress' — here مدار functions as an archaic/prohibitive verb form attached to رنجه ('do not cause suffering'). Altogether: 'Do not thus / do not again distress your tongue.'
segments.s649-g269: پدر‌گر is a conditional phrase (پدر + گر) meaning “if the father…”. نَکرد is the negated past of کردن (‘did not do’). آنچه is a relative pronoun ‘that which/what’. بایست + کرد is a periphrastic construction where بایست ('ought/must') precedes کرد ('did'), giving the sense ‘ought to have done’ or ‘what he ought to do’. Word order is typical classical Persian: conditional particle attached to the noun, then clause verb.
segments.s649-g270: ز here is the poetic preposition ‘because of/from’. مرگش is ‘his death’ (مرگ + enclitic -ش). پسر is the subject ‘the son’. گرم is an adjective meaning ‘fervent/zealous’ used predicatively about the son. و is the coordinating 'and'. تیمار خورد is an idiomatic sequence: تیمار ‘care/charge’ + خورد (here ‘to befall/receive’), meaning ‘received/took on the care’ or ‘assumed the responsibility.’
segments.s649-g271: تو را places ‘you’ with the enclitic را which marks a definite/direct object or gives emphasis/dative sense (‘to/for you’). من is the subject ‘I’. به سان یکی is a simile ‘like/as one (a single)’. بنده‌ام is بنده + ـام, present/continuous copula ‘I am a servant’ (humble self-reference). The overall order is topical (تو را) then subject then predicate simile.
segments.s649-g272: به پیش تو اندر is a prepositional phrase meaning ‘in your presence’ or ‘before you’ (به پیش + اندر intensifying the locative). پرستنده‌ام is پرستنده + ـام, ‘I am a worshipper/devotee’ (present-tense copula attached to the noun). The verb/copula is enclitic −ام; the phrase expresses humble devotion in the addressee’s presence.
segments.s649-g241: ورا is the older/poetic object form (او را) 'him' placed before the verb for emphasis. برگزیدند and بنشاندند are past, 3rd-person-plural transitive verbs coordinated by و; the sequence means 'they chose (him) and they installed (him)'. The double verbal coordination is straightforward narrative past.
segments.s649-g242: به شاهی is a prepositional phrase meaning 'into/onto kingship' (indicating elevation to the throne). بر او آفرین خواندند literally 'upon him acclamation they-called' — بر introduces the target ('upon him'), آفرین is the acclamation, and خواندند is past 3pl 'they uttered/called' (here 'they cried out [acclamation]'). Word order (prepositional phrase + preposition + object + noun + verb) reflects poetic inversion but is grammatically regular.
segments.s649-g243: به آهن here indicates the instrument/means 'with/in iron' (classical use of به). ببستند is past 3pl 'they bound/tied' and takes پای قباد ('the foot of Qobad') as its object; Persian places the possessed noun before the possessor (پای قباد = Qobad's foot).
segments.s649-g244: ز here = poetic از 'from/on account of' and introduces the things they ignored: فر و نژادش. یاد نکردند is the common light-verb construction ('یاد کردن' = to remember), with the noun یاد placed before the verb and نکردند (negated past 3pl) giving 'they did not remember'. The sequence means 'they did not take heed of / did not recall his glory and lineage.'
segments.s649-g245: چنینست is a contracted/classical copular 'چنین است' = 'it is thus'. رسم سرای کهن is a genitive/attributive phrase 'the custom of the ancient سرای'; in poetry the ezafe linking may be reduced or elided, so the nouns sit adjacent. The phrase means 'the custom of the ancient household/household order.'
segments.s649-g246: سرش is the possessed noun 'its head/top' with the enclitic -ش 'its'. هیـچ ... نبینی is a negative clause: هیـچ (here 'never' / 'no ... at all') + پیدا (apparent/visible) + نبینی (2nd-sg present-subjunctive/negative of دیدن used generically) — together 'you will not at all see [it] visible'. ز بن 'from the root' gives the source point. The order (adjective/noun 'پیدا' before the verb 'نبینی') is typical poetic syntax.
segments.s649-g247: یکی پور ... را گزین is an instruction/general prescription: یکی 'a certain/one', پور 'son (of)', followed by the name/epithet بد سوفزا and the object marker را; گزین is the root/imperative-form of 'to choose' used here as 'pick/select'. The word order places the object phrase before the verb (common in Persian) and the را marks it as definite/specific.
segments.s649-g248: A string of adjectives describes the chosen son: خردمند 'wise', پاکیزه 'pure/clean', and بآفرین literally 'with praise' (i.e., praiseworthy / celebrated). The coordinating و links them; there is no overt copula — the adjectives modify the previously mentioned object.
segments.s649-g185: This is a verbless nominal clause with prepositional/possessive phrases fronted: 'ز' (poetic 'from') introduces the source(s) — 'یزدان' (God) and the following particle 'وز' is a fused form (و + ز) meaning 'and from' in classical usage. 'لشکرم' = 'my army/host' (لشکر + possessive -م). The predicate 'نیست' (is not) follows, and the head noun 'شرم' (shame) is placed last: literally, 'From God and from my host there is no shame' = 'I have no shame before God or my host.'
segments.s649-g186: 'که' introduces the subordinate clause ('that'); 'من' is the subject 'I'; 'چند' functions adverbially here meaning 'so many / how many times' (i.e. 'so often'); 'پالوده‌ام' is perfective 1sg ('I have [been] پالوده')—in classical Persian پالودن can mean to stain/cover/drench (here: with blood); 'خون گرم' is the object noun phrase 'warm/fresh blood' with 'گرم' modifying 'خون'. Together: 'that I have so often been stained/drenched with warm blood.'
segments.s649-g187: 'بدانگه' is a temporal adverb 'at that time/thereupon'; 'کجا' here is a relative/locative 'where'; the clause 'شاه در بند بود' is literal 'the king was in bond(s)/chains' (در + بند = 'in fetters'); 'بود' is past tense copula 'was'. Word order places the temporal and locative before the predication.
segments.s649-g188: 'به یزدان' is a prepositional oath formula 'by God'; 'مرا' is a dative/experiential clitic marking the person bound by the oath ('to me' / 'I was bound'); 'سخت سوگند' is the nominal phrase 'a/firm solemn oath'; 'بود' is the past copula, so the literal structure 'to God to-me a strong oath was' = 'I had solemnly sworn by God.' This is a classical Persian construction where سوگند بودن expresses 'to be under an oath'.
segments.s649-g189: 'که' again introduces a dependent/jussive clause expressing a wish or oath; 'دستم' = 'my hand' (possessive -م). The verb 'نبیند' is negative subjunctive/jussive 'may not see / may not meet' used here as a wish ('may my hand see/encounter nothing except...'). 'مگر' = 'except' and 'دست تیغ' = 'the hand of the sword' (a personifying/metaphorical expression for the sword's hilt/force). So the clause means 'may my hand encounter none but the sword's hand.'
segments.s649-g190: This is a separate action clause: 'به جنگ آفتاب' = 'into the sunlit battle' or 'into the battle like the sun' (prepositional phrase modifying the verb). 'اندر آرم' uses classical اندر + 'آرم' (1sg) — here 'آرم' functions poetically as 'I put/bring/plant' (classical uses allow 'set/enter'); 'به میغ' is instrumental/associative 'with the blade.' The whole means 'I will set/drive my blade into the sunlit fight.'
segments.s649-g191: 'مگر' here introduces an exception/conditional 'unless'; 'سر دهم‌گر' is a fused conditional 'دهم' (I give) + 'گر' (if) yielding 'if I give (my) head'. The repeated 'سر' is the direct object 'head' (emphatic). 'خوشنواز' is adjectival/figurative here meaning 'pleasing' or 'delightful' (literally 'melodious') rather than a literal 'musician' — so the phrase reads roughly 'unless I give my head, if the head would be pleasing.' This is an elliptical conditional tied to what follows.
segments.s649-g192: 'به مردی' marks the affected person 'upon/against a man' (i.e. a man as object/target); 'ز تخت' = 'from the throne' (source); 'اندر آرم' again uses 'آرم' (1sg) 'I set/put/throw into' in a classical poetic sense; 'به گاز' is an adverbial idiom 'with a bite' (گاز 'bite'), i.e. 'in one swift stroke/at one gulp' — figuratively 'at a single fierce blow.' Together: 'I will cast a man from his throne in a single, swift (bite-like) blow.'
segments.s649-g369: This is a locative noun phrase. به ایران here functions poetically as a place-marker meaning 'in/within Iran'; همه modifies سالخورده ('all the aged'), and ردان is an archaic noun attached as the head of the phrase (here 'nobles/leders' is the sense imposed by context). This whole chunk supplies the subject/scene for the verb in the next segment.
segments.s649-g370: نشستند is the 3rd-person plural past of 'نشستن' (= 'they sat/assembled'); it governs the previous noun phrase as its subject. با is the preposition 'with', نامور is an adjective 'renowned' modifying the following noun, and بخردان is an old plural form meaning 'wise ones/wise men'. Together: 'they sat with the renowned wise men.'
segments.s649-g371: که introduces a subordinate clause ('that/so that'). این کار = 'this affair'; گردد is the optative/subjunctive 3rd singular of گردیدن meaning 'may become / may happen'. به ما marks the experiencer ('upon us' / 'to us'); بر دراز is a postpositional phrase where بر is the onto/upon marker and دراز ('long') indicates duration — 'becomes long upon us.'
segments.s649-g372: میان is the preposition 'between'; دو شهزاد = 'two princes' (the numeral is implicit in the word order), and گردن‌فراز is a postpositive adjective 'proud, high‑necked/boastful' describing the princes. Together they indicate the locus/party between whom the affair is prolonged.
segments.s649-g373: ز is the poetic form of از ('from'), fronting the origin phrases 'ز روم و ز چین' = 'from Rome and from China'. لشکر آید is a present/narrative-present clause 'an army comes' (آید is 3rd-sing present), and کنون is the temporal adverb 'now' placed after the verb for emphasis.
segments.s649-g374: بریزند is a 3rd-person plural subjunctive/optative form of ریختن used here like a future/intentional 'they will spill/pour out'; ز این مرز = 'from this frontier'; بسیار modifies خون quantitatively ('much/abundant blood'). The clause states the expected result: much blood will be shed at this border.
segments.s649-g375: بباید is an older/poetic form of باید expressing obligation ('must'/'ought to'); it governs the following verb. خرامید is the infinitive/imperative-type form of خرامیدن 'to stride/prance' and سوی قباد means 'toward Qobad' (سوی = toward(s), قباد = the king's name). Together: 'we/one must stride toward Qobad.'
segments.s649-g376: مگر introduces an exception ('unless'); کان is an archaic demonstrative/particle modifying سخنها ('those words/utterances'); نگیرد is the negative 3rd-singular present/subjunctive of گرفتن here with the sense 'take/come into' — in the fixed phrase نگیرد به یاد it means 'do not come to mind / are not remembered'. The clause thus qualifies the obligation: 'unless those words do not occur to the memory.'
segments.s649-g321: This clause narrates the action and the speech: بدو is 'to him/unto him' (prepositional), داد is the past simple 'gave', و گفت links the action to direct speech ('and said'), and the following short clause (این نگین را بدار) is the reported imperative. In the quoted imperative, این نگین را places the object with the definite object marker را, and بدار is an imperatival verb meaning 'hold/keep' (classical/poetic imperative form).
segments.s649-g322: This is a single temporal clause with two uses of بود. بود روز = 'there was a day' (existential/past 'was'); کاین را is a relative/ demonstrative 'when/that which (this)'; the second بود functions as an auxiliary/modal sense ('would be' / 'there will be'), and خواستار is the predicate meaning 'desirous' — together: 'a day will be when someone will be desirous of it.' The inverted classical word order places the temporal phrase before the verb.
segments.s649-g323: بدان ده here functions as 'to him (he) gave' (بدان = 'to him/unto that one', ده representing the giving action in classical sequence). یکی هفته = 'one week' (duration). از بهر ماه is the classical phrase 'از بهر' meaning 'for the sake of' + ماه 'month' — i.e. 'one week for that month' or 'a week's leave in that month.'
segments.s649-g324: همی‌بود is an imperfect/continuous past meaning 'he remained/it continued so.' و links to the next action. بیامد is the past simple 'came' (here 'set out/arrived'), and به راه is the prepositional phrase 'on the road' or 'onto the way' (به + راه). The sequence describes staying for a week and then departing on the eighth day.
segments.s649-g325: The clause has inverted classical order: بر شاه هیتال شد literally 'became/up (to) King Hital' — idiomatically 'he came up before King Hital' or 'he approached King Hital.' شد is the past/inchoative verb 'happened/became' used here to mark the arrival/appearance, and کیقباد is the person (Kay Qobad) placed after the verb in typical epic inversion.
segments.s649-g326: This clause uses the light-verb construction یاد کردن: گذشته سخنها = 'the speeches/tales of the past' (سخنها plural of 'speech/tale'), بدو = 'to him', کرد یاد = literally 'made remembrance' = 'reminded him.' The order 'کرد یاد' is an idiomatic poetic phrasing for 'recalled/mentioned.'
segments.s649-g327: بگفت is past 'he said/told' (the prefix ب- is an older narrative/form of the verb), آنچه = 'that which/what', کردند = past plural 'they did', ایرانیان = 'the Iranians' (subject). Together: 'He said what the Iranians had done.'
segments.s649-g328: بدی را = 'evil' with the object marker را. ببستند is past plural of بستن 'to bind/shut/secure' — here idiomatically 'they bound/suppressed' (i.e., they checked or restrained wrongdoing). یک‌یک = 'one by one/each in turn', میان = 'in the midst/among' (i.e. among themselves). The line says they removed or restrained evils one by one from among them.
segments.s649-g329: بدو گفت شاه: a standard classical Persian word order where the indirect object/goal (بدو, 'to him') precedes the verb گفت ('said') and the subject شاه ('the king') follows the verb. The phrase از بد خوشنواز functions as a vocative/epithet addressed to the addressee — خوشنواز ('melodious' or 'pleasant‑sounding') modifies بد (here likely a name or epithet); از is the simple preposition 'from/of' used in a fixed epithet formation.
segments.s649-g330: همانا بدین روزت آمد نیاز: همانا is an emphatic particle 'verily/truly'. بدین is the fused prepositional expression meaning 'on/at this' (ب + دین in the manuscript split), followed by روزت 'your day/time' (روز + enclitic -ت), then the past tense آمد 'came'. نیاز here (نیاز آمد) is an idiomatic noun phrase meaning 'the time/occasion of supplication/need has come' — a perfunctory perfective statement: 'your time to ask/pray has come.'
segments.s649-g331: به پیمان سپارم تو را لشکری: سپارم is the 1st‑person singular present/subjunctive of سپردن 'to entrust/leave', here used as a promise 'I will entrust'. به پیمان ('by/under a pact' or 'by covenant') indicates the terms; تو را (with object marker را) is the indirect object 'to you' (dative). لشکری (an indeclinable/nisba form) is the thing being entrusted, 'an army/forces'. Word order places the prepositional phrase and verb before the indirect object and the object noun for emphasis.
segments.s649-g332: از آن هر یکی بر سران افسری: از آن 'from those (men/units)' selects the source. هر یکی 'each one' is distributive. بر سران افسری literally 'upon the heads (سران) an افسری' — افسری here most likely names a diadem/helmet (a noun), so the phrase means 'place a diadem/helmet on each chief's head'. The sequence is typical poetic Persian: source → distributive numeral → locative phrase describing allocation.
segments.s649-g333: که‌گر باز یابی تو گنج و کلاه: که‌گر is the conditional 'if'. باز یابی is the 2nd‑person verb form meaning 'you find/shall find' with باز 'again' preceding it; the subject pronoun تو is explicit after the verb (classical emphasis/order). گنج و کلاه are the direct objects 'treasure and helmet/hat' (کلاه functioning here as a helmet/war‑cap in context). The clause sets a hypothetical future condition.
segments.s649-g334: چغانی بباشد تو را نیکخواه: چغانی is a nisba/adjective (a person 'of Chaghān' or a tribal name) used substantively here ('a Chaghani'); بباشد is a subjunctive/wishful use of بودن 'to be' (classical optative 'may there be' / 'will be'), followed by the dative تو را 'for you' and the predicate نیکخواه 'well‑wisher/benefactor'. The clause expresses the hoped‑for result: 'a Chaghani will be your friend/well‑wisher.'
segments.s649-g335: مرا باشد این مرز و فرمان تو را: مرا (for me) + باشد (subjunctive/present of بودن used here with future/possessive force) marks possession/assignment: 'this border and command shall be for me' (i.e. 'will belong to me'). این مرز و فرمان is the nominal phrase 'this frontier and command/authority'. The trailing تو را is emphatic/dative 'for you' — in classical Persian such double datives or resumptive pronouns can add emphasis or mark the person concerned; the clause should be read as an allocation/statement of who gets what.
segments.s649-g336: ز کرده نباشد پشیمان تو را: ز 'from/because of' + کرده (participle of کردن 'done') forms 'from what has been done'. نباشد is the negative subjunctive/optative 'may there not be / you shall not be', and پشیمان 'regretful' is the predicative adjective; تو را again marks the person affected. Together: 'May you not be regretful because of what has been done' → 'you will not regret it.'
segments.s649-g33: This is a nominal clause with a past copula: ستون (head noun 'pillar') + خرد (postposed modifier 'of wisdom') form the subject 'the pillar of wisdom'. بردباری is a predicate noun 'patience' and بود is the past copula 'was', linking subject and predicate.
segments.s649-g34: چو introduces a temporal/conditional clause ('when'); تندی کند is a verb phrase where تندی ('haste') is personified and کند ('makes/does') is a present/habitual verb, and the main clause تن به خواری بود uses تن as subject, به خواری as a prepositional phrase 'into/unto humiliation', and بود as the copula in the past narrative tense.
segments.s649-g35: چو 'when' introduces the temporal clause. خرسند گشتی is a verbal phrase 'you became/are pleased' (گشتی is a past/cessative form used here for 'became/are'), and به داد خدای is a prepositional phrase 'by/to the justice (help) of God', marking the means or source of the resulting state.
segments.s649-g36: توانگر شدی is a compound predicate 'you became wealthy' where شدی is the past of 'become'. The adjectives یکدل و پاکرای follow as predicate complements linked by و 'and' describing the resulting moral qualities.
segments.s649-g37: گر is the conditional 'if'. آزاد داری is a transitive verbal phrase 'you keep (it) free' or 'you hold (it) free', with تنت را as the definite direct object ('your body' + object marker را). ز رنج specifies 'from suffering/ hardship'.
segments.s649-g38: This is a nominal sentence with an implied copula: تن مرد بی‌رنج is a descriptive noun phrase ('a man whose body is free from suffering' or 'a pain-free body of a man'), and بهتر ز گنج is the predicate 'is better than treasure' (بهتر = 'better', ز = 'than/from'). The copula 'is' is omitted as common in Persian poetry.
segments.s649-g39: هر آن کس که is a universal-relative construction 'everyone who/ whoever'. بخشش کند is the verb phrase 'does a giving/ makes a donation' (بخشش here = 'donation/ almsgiving') and با کسی marks the beneficiary 'to/with someone'.
segments.s649-g40: بمیرد is a third-person verbal form 'dies' (used here as general/hypothetical). تنش is 'his body' (تن + enclitic ش 'his'), and the following clause نام ماند بسی has نام as subject 'name/reputation' and ماند 'remains'; بسی is an adverb 'much/greatly' (emphatic). The sentence contrasts bodily death with lasting fame.
segments.s649-g409: نهاد is the past (3rd sg.) of نهادن here, ‘placed/established’. The verb comes first (poetic order) and is followed by the locative phrase اندر آن مرز and the noun آتشکده as the thing established; اندر = 'in/within'.
segments.s649-g410: بزرگی qualifies the preceding آتشکده (a large/great fire‑temple); به is the preposition 'for' indicating occasion, introducing the festivals نوروز and جشن سده (Sadeh festival). The compound expresses purpose/occasions for which the temple was set up.
segments.s649-g411: مداین is the place name (Madā'in). پی here is an adverb 'then/after' and افگند is the past of افکندن 'he set/laid/founded'; جای کیان is the complement 'the place/seat of the Kians'. Word order places the place name first, then the temporal adverb and verb, then the complement.
segments.s649-g412: پراگنده is an adjective/adverb 'widespread, scattered' describing the realm; بسیار quantifies the following nouns meaning 'much/very much'; سود و زیان = 'profit and loss'. Classical Persian often omits a copula, so this reads as '[it] was widespread and [brought] much profit and loss.'
segments.s649-g413: از ... تا is a locative span 'from Ahvaz to Pars' (تا = 'as far as'). یک شارستان = 'one/a city' (object/complement), placed after the locative phrase and linked to the following verb.
segments.s649-g414: بکرد is the past tense of کردن here meaning 'he built/made'. The clause continues with و (and) plus برآورد, a verb meaning 'he set up/established' (برآورد بیمارستان = 'established a hospital'), with بیمارستان as the direct object of برآورد.
segments.s649-g415: اراتن (اران) is the name assigned; خواند here means 'called/named' (not 'read'). آن شارستان را is the definite object marked by را ('that city' as the thing named). قباد at the end is the agent/subject: 'Qobad called that city Aran.' Poetic word order places the name at the start and the subject at the end.
segments.s649-g416: که introduces a relative/subordinate clause 'which'. تازی here means 'the Arabs' (ethnonym), کنون = 'now'; نام ... نهاد is an idiom 'to give a name' or 'named it' (نام = 'name' as the object, نهاد = past of نهادن 'placed/gave'). The clause thus says the Arabs have (now) called it Halwan.
segments.s649-g401: وز آن پس = a temporal/connecting phrase (‘and/after that’). آن پس is an adverbial sequence meaning ‘after that/then’. بیاورد is a third-person past (classical/nearly archaic) form meaning ‘brought’ (here simple past). لشکر is the direct object ‘army/host’, and به روم is a prepositional phrase marking destination (‘to Rome’). The verb appears before its object and PP here (poetic word order), but functions as the main past action.
segments.s649-g402: شد is a simple past/preterite marker here meaning ‘it happened/it came about’. آن باره is a temporal noun phrase ‘that occasion/that moment’. او is the third-person subject ‘he’. چو introduces a simile ‘like/as’. یک مهره موم is a noun phrase ‘a bead (mohre) of wax’ (مهره modified by موم). The clause means ‘on that occasion he was like a wax bead’; چو + NP forms the comparison.
segments.s649-g403: همه بوم و بر is a fixed pair meaning ‘the whole land/realm’ (بوم و بر = land and region). آتش اندر زدند is a verbal phrase ‘they put/set (it) on fire’ (آتش + اندر + زدند = ‘set fire within/to it’). زدند is third-person plural past. Word order places the whole-land phrase fronted as the affected domain.
segments.s649-g404: همه رومیان is the subject ‘all the Romans’. دست بر سر زدند is an idiomatic verb phrase: دست بر سر زدن = ‘to strike/beat one’s hand on one’s head’ (an act of mourning/grief). زدند is plural past; بر is the preposition ‘upon’ and سر is the object of that preposition (‘head’).
segments.s649-g405: همی‌کرد is an imperfective/habitual past form (‘was doing’ / ‘would do’), often used in epic narration. زان = ‘from that’ / ‘therefrom’ (referring back to the previously mentioned place or circumstance). بوم و بر خارستان is the noun phrase ‘that land and region (بوم و بر) — a thorny place’ (خارستان = ‘thorny thicket’). The clause describes ongoing action directed at that territory.
segments.s649-g406: ازو خواست = ‘he asked of him / he requested from him’ (ازو = ‘from him’, خواست = past of خواستن ‘to ask/want’). زنهار here is a noun/exclamation ‘a warning / “beware!”’ used as the content of the request; دو شارستان = ‘the two towns/cities’. Grammatically, خواست takes ازو as its indirect object and زنهار دو شارستان as the thing requested (a warning concerning the two towns).
segments.s649-g407: یکی ... و دگر ... is a simple nominative juxtaposition meaning ‘one (was) X and the other Y’ (یکی = ‘one’, دگر = ‘the other’). مندیا and فارقین are proper names (here place-names). This segment lists the two towns previously referred to.
segments.s649-g408: بیامختشان is a past transitive verb ‘he taught them’ formed from بیاموختن with the enclitic pronoun -شان (‘them’) attached. زند here is a noun (Zand — the Zoroastrian commentary/text) rather than a verb, so the sequence reads ‘he taught them (the) Zend’. بنهاد is past tense ‘he founded/established’ (بنهاد = بنا نهاد, poetic past), and دین is the object ‘religion’. Word order: verb + object (+ conjunction) + verb + object.
segments.s649-g137: This is a coordinated clause: تویی (second-person copula 'you are') + باژخواه (a nominal predicate 'extortioner/toll‑seeker'). The conjunction و connects a second clause: منم is the first‑person copula 'I am' (emphatic because of the -م), followed by the prepositional phrase با گناه 'with sin' (= 'guilty'). Word order is Subject–Predicate (copula before predicate) and the preposition با introduces the accompanying quality.
segments.s649-g138: نخواهم is a negative volitive/optative in the 1st person ('I do not wish / I will not'), followed by the complementizer که introducing a subjunctive clause. خوانی is 2nd‑person present-subjunctive of خواندن here meaning 'call / name / summon'. مرا را is the object 'me' plus the object marker را (the data splits مرا as م + را); مرا (م + را) is the direct object and نیز شاه places the predicative noun 'king' after the object: 'that you call me king also.'
segments.s649-g139: فرستادم is a simple past 1st‑person transitive verb 'I sent'. اینک is an adverb 'now / behold' modifying the action. یکی پهلوان is an indefinite noun phrase 'a certain champion' (یکی before the noun gives the sense 'one / a certain').
segments.s649-g140: ز کردار تو is a prepositional phrase 'because of / on account of your deeds' (ز = 'from / because of', کردار = 'deed(s)', تو = 'your/you'). چند is an interrogative/indefinite adverb 'how much / to what extent', followed by باشم, the 1st‑person present-subjunctive of بودن 'that I may be / that I might be'; نوان here functions as a predicative adjective 'new / renewed' (poetic/older usage), so the cluster expresses 'to see, because of your deeds, how I might be (or how much I may be renewed / what I might amount to).' Grammatically, چند + باشم forms an interrogative subordinate clause governed by the verb of sending.
segments.s649-g141: چو ... باشد is a conditional clause 'if/when ... is'. Here چو introduces the condition ('if'), نامه is the subject 'letter', بدین‌گونه is an adverbial 'in this manner / of this sort', and باشد is the present-subjunctive/optative 3rd‑person 'it be / may be'. The word بدوی in the manuscript is archaic/poetic and functions here as a direction or address related to delivery; its lexical senses in the provided list do not match this usage (see senses field).
segments.s649-g142: This clause uses چو again as 'like / such as' to introduce an example or comparison: چو من 'such as me / like me' (من = 'I/me'). دشمن is a nominative noun 'an enemy' describing that person, and لشکری جنگجوی is a noun phrase 'a warlike army' (لشکری = 'army/troops', followed by the adjective/epithet جنگجوی 'warlike, warriorly'). The structure lists recipients/targets: someone like me (an enemy) and a fighting army.
segments.s649-g143: نمانم is the negative 1st‑person present/subjunctive of ماندن 'I will not stay / I will not remain (passive)'. که introduces the subordinate clause describing what one will not tolerate. بر هم زند is a verbal phrase meaning 'to throw into confusion / to break up / to disturb' (بر هم + زند where زند is the verbal element); thus the clause means 'I will not remain (passive) that he disturb [things].' نیز چشم here literally 'also eye' — in context چشم functions idiomatically with nearby verbs to indicate looking/inspection or attention, and is tied to the sense 'I will not keep my eyes passive / I will not stand by so that he may make trouble.' Note: the manuscript verb-token زند has no matching sense in the provided list and is treated accordingly.
segments.s649-g144: نگویم is the 1st‑person negative present/subjunctive of گفتن 'I do not / will not say' (the provided lexical senses give 'conversation/discussion' rather than the verb form; see senses field). سخن پیش او 'a word before him / in his presence' uses سخن 'word/speech' + پیش 'before/in front of' + او 'him'. جز introduces an exception 'except', and به خشم means 'in anger' (به + خشم = 'with/by anger'). The whole clause: 'I will say no words before him except in anger.'
segments.s649-g105: هم is an adverb meaning “also/at the same time.” اندر زمان is a temporal prepositional phrase ‘at that time’ (اندر = archaic/in poetic use of از/در). برنشاند is a past transitive verb (he seated/placed on a seat) and ورا is the enclitic object meaning ‘him/to him’ placed after the verb.
segments.s649-g106: ز ری سوی درگاه is a chain of prepositional phrases: ز ری ‘from Rey’ + سوی درگاه ‘toward the court/into the sovereign’s presence.’ خواند is a past tense verb here meaning ‘called/summoned,’ and ورا again is the object ‘him.’ The order is typical poetic Persian: prepositional elements precede the verb, and the object-clitic follows it.
segments.s649-g107: دو اسبه فرستاده is a compound: دو (numeral) + اسبه (steeds/horses) with فرستاده qualifying them as ‘sent/messengers’ — i.e. two horse-mounted messengers. آمد is the past-tense verb ‘came/arrived,’ and به ری is the locative phrase ‘to/at Rey.’
segments.s649-g108: چو introduces a simile here (‘like/as’). باد خزانی is the noun phrase ‘autumnal wind.’ به هنگام is a fixed temporal phrase ‘at the time of/at the moment of,’ and دی here is an archaic temporal word indicating the time of day (contextually ‘dusk’/late day). The whole phrase forms a simile comparing the arrival to an autumn wind at the late hour.
segments.s649-g109: چو is the subordinating conjunction ‘when.’ دیدش is the past verb دید (saw) with the enclitic -ش ‘him’ as object. بپرسید is the past-tense verb ‘he asked.’ سالار is the subject noun ‘the commander/chief,’ and بار here means ‘load/burden’ (i.e. the cargo or matter being asked about).
segments.s649-g110: وز is an archaic/poetic form functioning as the preposition ‘from’ (essentially = از here) — this form is not captured by the provided nominal senses list. او is ‘him,’ بستد is a past verb here meaning ‘bound/sealed/took into custody’ (sense ‘bind/tie’ or ‘seize’), and آن نامه شهریار is the noun phrase ‘that letter of the king’ (آن = that, نامه = letter, شهریار = king).
segments.s649-g111: بیامد is the simple past ‘he came.’ به شاپور رازی is the prepositional phrase ‘to Shapur Razi’ (رازی here functioning as an epithet ‘of Rey’ / part of the name). سپرد is a past transitive verb meaning ‘he entrusted/handed over’ (i.e. delivered it to the named person).
segments.s649-g112: سوار سرافراز is the noun phrase ‘the honored/proud rider’ (سرافراز qualifying سوار). را is the definite object marker attached to the phrase. پیش برد is a compound verbal idea: پیش (here ‘before/in the presence of’) + برد (verb ‘to bring/lead’), so together ‘brought forward/led into the presence.’
segments.s649-g17: هر آن گه که شد ... is a temporal clause meaning “whenever/at the time that … became.” The sequence هر آن گه (literally “every that occasion”) = “whenever.” شد is the verbal past/decisive sense “became.” پادشا کژ گوی is a predicate nominal: پادشا (king) + کژ گوی (literally “crooked-speaking,” i.e. one who speaks perversely or falsely). In classical Persian the copula can be expressed by شد + noun phrase (“became X”).
segments.s649-g18: ز کژی شود شاه پیکارجوی is a causal-result clause: ز (because/of) + کژی (the crookedness) names the cause; شود (becomes) is the verb; شاه is the subject and پیکارجوی is the resulting predicate adjective ‘seeking battle’ or ‘belligerent.’ Poetic word order puts شود before شاه for meter/emphasis but the meaning is “because of that crookedness the king becomes belligerent.”
segments.s649-g19: سخن را بباید شنیدن نخست uses the construction بباید + infinitive to express obligation: “one must/ought to hear.” سخن را (with the object marker را) is placed before the verb; شنیدن is the infinitive “to hear,” and نخست (first) is an adverbial qualifier. The whole reads “One must first hear the speech/what is said.”
segments.s649-g20: چو دانا شود is another temporal clause: چو = “when,” دانا = “the wise/knowing one,” شود = “becomes / is present.” پاسخ آید درست literally “an answer comes (آید) correct (درست),” i.e. a proper/correct reply arrives when the wise person is there. Note the simple present/habitual sense of آید in classical style.
segments.s649-g21: چو داننده مردم بود آزور is a conditional/temporal construction: چو = “when/if,” داننده مردم = “the people's knower” or “the sage of the people,” بود = past copula “was/if he were,” and آزور is a poetic adjective meaning “azure / lapis-lazuli” used metaphorically for splendid or conspicuous appearance. The clause says ‘if/when the people's sage is splendid/azure…’
segments.s649-g22: همی دانش او نیاید به بر: همی here is an adverbial emphatic “still / even so.” دانش او = “his knowledge.” نیاید is the classical negative-present form of آمدن meaning “does not come / does not appear / does not avail.” به بر is an idiomatic locution meaning “to his side/benefit” (i.e. to his aid). Together: “Even so, his knowledge will not come to his aid.”
segments.s649-g23: هر آن گه که دانا بود پرشتاب repeats the temporal formula “whenever” (هر آن گه که) + شدن/بود: دانا بود = “the wise man was,” and پرشتاب is an adjectival predicate “hurried / hasty.” So: “Whenever the wise man was hasty…” The phrase sets up a contrast in the next segment.
segments.s649-g24: چه ... چه ... here lists alternatives (“whether … or …”). The first چه introduces دانش مر او را = “(whether) [it is] knowledge for/to him” (مر is an older object/pronominal form ‘me/to me’ used here idiomatically), and the second چه introduces در سر شراب = “or wine in his head” (در سر = ‘in the head,’ i.e. in his mind). The poet means: when a supposed wise man is rash, one cannot tell whether his mind holds knowledge or drunkenness.
segments.s649-g177: به مردی is an appositive/preposed phrase meaning “a man” (lit. “as a man” or “a man”). رهانیدم is a simple past first‑person verb “I freed.” او را is the object pronoun with the definite/object marker را, and ز بند is a poetic prepositional phrase “from (his) bond(s)” using the archaic short form ز instead of از. Word order: ‘‘[apposition] + verb + object + prepositional phrase.’’
segments.s649-g178: نماندم is past negative first person ‘‘I did not remain/linger’’ (ن‑ negative + ماندم). که introduces a subordinate clause ‘‘that/for’’ and here has a causal/optative force. آید is a present/subjunctive form used to express potential or event that might happen (“that harm should come/come to pass”). به رویش is a dative/locative phrase “upon his face” (به + روی + possessive enclitic ش), and گزند is the noun “harm.”
segments.s649-g179: مرا داستان بود is a common Classical Persian construction meaning literally “to me a story was,” i.e. “I had a tale / there was a tale for me.” The personal pronoun is expressed as مرا (here split in the gloss), داستان is the noun “story/tale,” and بود is the past copula “was.” نزدیک شاه is a locative phrase “near the king.”
segments.s649-g180: همان نزد is an adverbial/locative bundle meaning “there/at that very place / in the presence of.” گردان here means “troop/unit,” and ایران سپاه is a genitive chain meaning “the army of Iran” or “Iran’s army.” Note the poetic inversion: Persian would more prosaically have سپاه ایران گردان, but poets freely rearrange the elements.
segments.s649-g181: گر is the conditional conjunction “if.” ایدون is an archaic/poetic verb meaning roughly “it appears / it proves” (here ‘if it proves/turns out’). که introduces the clause ‘‘that…’’ بندست is a copular statement composed of بند (fetter/chain) + است (contracted) meaning “is a fetter.” پاداش من is the genitive phrase “my reward.” Overall: “If it turns out that it is a fetter, my reward…”
segments.s649-g182: تو را is the direct object “you” with the object marker را. چنگ دادن is a verbal phrase literally “to give a claw” but idiomatically “to seize/assail”; here دادن functions as the light verb making a verbal phrase. به پرخاش من means “with my reproach / by means of my reproach,” so the whole segment means “to seize/assail you with my reproach.”
segments.s649-g183: نخواهم is the negative/future sense of خواستن here (“I will not / I do not wish”). زمان can be read adverbially as “at any time / for a period,” and از تو gives the reason/source “because of you / from you.” پایم is “my foot” (پای + enclitic ـم) and ببند is the present/subjunctive or jussive form of بستن “to bind/fasten” (here functionally “bind”). The clause expresses refusal to allow time/occasion (or delay) to bind his feet because of that person.
segments.s649-g184: بدارد here is an optative/jussive form meaning roughly “may it hold / let it have” (from the root دار/داشتن in an older idiom). مرا (split in the gloss as م + را) is the object “me.” بند is “fetter,” او is the third‑person pronoun “his/its,” and سودمند is the adjective “useful/beneficial.” The phrase therefore says “let his fetter, if it takes me, be advantageous,” i.e. accepting his bond only if it proves useful.
